title: Abdominal ectopic pregnancy
summary: Abdominal ectopic pregnancy (AEP) is a rare form of ectopic pregnancy in which the gestational sac implants outside the fallopian tube, typically in the peritoneal cavity, omentum, or other intra-abdominal organs including the spleen, intestine, and liver. When diagnosed after 20 weeks' gestation, AEP presents a significant clinical emergency due to abnormal placental implantation, which can cause maternal-fetal mortality, major obstetric hemorrhage, and coagulopathy. Patients typically present with heavy vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain, and diagnosis is supported by elevated beta-human chorionic gonadotropin levels and transvaginal ultrasound, with laparoscopy providing confirmatory visualization. Management options include both surgical interventions such as laparoscopy or laparotomy and medical therapies including methotrexate or potassium chloride injection. Recognition of clinical presentation and prompt diagnostic confirmation are essential for preventing catastrophic maternal complications associated with this rare pregnancy complication.

title: A rare case of infection and spontaneous rupture in ovarian mucinous cystadenoma
summary: This case report describes a rare clinical scenario in which an ovarian mucinous cystadenoma became infected, possibly secondary to pelvic inflammatory disease, and subsequently ruptured spontaneously. The infection and rupture of the cyst led to acute peritonitis and sepsis in a young, non-pregnant woman presenting with acute abdominal symptoms. The pathological progression—from pelvic inflammatory disease to cyst infection to spontaneous rupture—demonstrates an uncommon but serious complication of ovarian cystic lesions. While endometriotic cysts and other common pelvic pathologies were considered in the differential diagnosis, they were ruled out in this particular case. This report highlights the importance of recognizing that mucinous cystadenomas, typically considered benign and stable lesions, can become life-threatening when infected and rupture. The case underscores the critical need for clinical vigilance in evaluating acute peritonitis in women with known or suspected ovarian cysts.

title: Glutathione-Responsive Methotrexate Polymersomes for Potential Management of Ectopic Pregnancy
summary: This study presents glutathione-responsive polymersomes as an improved delivery system for methotrexate (MTX) in treating ectopic pregnancy. MTX is the standard first-line chemotherapeutic agent that inhibits folate-dependent nucleic acid synthesis, causing destruction of rapidly dividing ectopic trophoblast tissue. However, rapid drug clearance limits MTX accumulation at ectopic implantation sites, requiring high doses that cause adverse effects including nausea, vomiting, bone marrow suppression, and interstitial pneumonitis. The researchers developed MTX-loaded disulfide-containing polymersomes (MTX-SS-Ps) composed of a PEG-polycaprolactone copolymer with 38.2 nm diameter spherical particles that preferentially accumulate in uterine implantation sites while avoiding fetal tissue. Intracellular glutathione reduces the disulfide bonds in the polymersomes, triggering efficient drug release at the implantation site and achieving a 6-fold dose reduction compared to free MTX while demonstrating no hepatic, renal, or cardiac toxicity. In mice, MTX-SS-Ps induced complete pregnancy resorption and fetal development inhibition, whereas free MTX at the same clinically recommended dose showed no effect, establishing this glutathione-responsive nanocarrier as a superior targeted therapeutic approach for ectopic pregnancy management.

title: Rare case of haemoperitoneum secondary to a ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancy superimposed by a bleeding corpus luteum cyst
summary: This case report describes a rare clinical presentation combining a ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancy with a concurrent bleeding corpus luteum cyst, both of which triggered acute abdominal pain and haemoperitoneum in a female patient. Both ectopic pregnancies and haemorrhagic corpus luteal cysts are recognized causes of acute pelvic pain in women of childbearing age, but their simultaneous occurrence is exceptionally uncommon. The diagnosis was facilitated by elevated beta-human chorionic gonadotropin levels, which are characteristically associated with ectopic pregnancy. Diagnostic laparoscopy served as the primary treatment modality and proved to be a safe and effective management strategy for addressing the ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancy. This case highlights the importance of considering ruptured corpus luteal cysts and ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancies as rare but critical differential diagnoses in women presenting with acute abdominal pain. The findings underscore the clinical value of combining biochemical markers with minimally invasive surgical evaluation in managing complex gynecological emergencies.

title: Nanomedicines for Improved Management of Ectopic Pregnancy: A Narrative Review
summary: Ectopic pregnancy remains a critical clinical emergency, representing the leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality due to its potential to progress to tubal rupture and life-threatening hemorrhage. While methotrexate effectively treats ectopic pregnancies, its use is limited by significant adverse effects including alopecia, photosensitivity, gastrointestinal disturbances, thrombocytopenia, and rare but serious complications such as hepatotoxicity and pneumonitis. This review examines emerging nanomedicine approaches to improve ectopic pregnancy management through enhanced diagnostic biomarker detection, including iron oxide nanoparticles and graphene-based biosensors that enable ultra-sensitive measurement of hCG and pregnancy-associated plasma proteins, as well as microRNA panels like miR-323-3p that show promise for differentiating ectopic from intrauterine pregnancies. Advanced imaging modalities utilizing nanoparticles—including perfluoropropane and mesoporous silica-based ultrasound contrast agents, photoacoustic imaging with PEG-PCL nanoparticles, and near-infrared fluorescence imaging with silicon naphthalocyanine dyes—offer improved visualization of pregnancy localization and placental monitoring while minimizing fetal toxicity. Together, these nanomedicine strategies represent a transformative approach to ectopic pregnancy management by enabling earlier, more accurate diagnosis and safer monitoring compared to conventional methotrexate-based treatment.

title: A Rare Case of Spontaneous Heterotopic Pregnancy at 12 weeks of Gestation Following Natural Conception With Literature Review
summary: Heterotopic pregnancy, the simultaneous occurrence of intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies, shares similar risk factors with ectopic pregnancy alone, including pelvic inflammatory disease, Chlamydia infection, endometriosis, smoking, intrauterine devices, and pelvic adhesions. The increasing use of assisted reproductive technologies and ovulation-stimulating medications has elevated the incidence of heterotopic pregnancy, potentially through mechanisms involving ovulatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with abdominal or pelvic pain and vaginal bleeding, and diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound and 2D/3D imaging to identify both intrauterine and ectopic gestations, as elevated β-hCG levels alone cannot distinguish between these conditions. Surgical management via laparoscopic salpingectomy is required to remove the ectopic pregnancy while preserving the viable intrauterine pregnancy, followed by progesterone support to maintain the intrauterine gestation. This case highlights the critical importance of comprehensive imaging evaluation in all pregnant patients presenting with acute abdominal pain to prevent missed diagnoses and ensure optimal maternal and fetal outcomes.

title: Case report: Successful laparoscopic management of early abdominal pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

Abdominal pregnancy, characterized by implantation of the gestational sac within the peritoneal cavity, is a rare but life-threatening variant of ectopic pregnancy associated with a maternal mortality rate of 5.1 per 1,000 pregnancies. This case report demonstrates successful laparoscopic management of early abdominal pregnancy in a 33-year-old patient who presented with abdominal pain and was diagnosed through transvaginal ultrasonography combined with elevated serum β-hCG levels. Risk factors for abdominal pregnancy include prior ectopic pregnancy, pelvic surgery, infertility history, and assisted reproductive technology, with some cases potentially linked to endometriosis. The patient underwent laparoscopic exploration revealing a 500 mL pelvic hematoma and active bleeding from the right uterosacral ligament; the gestational tissue was successfully excised, and histopathological analysis confirmed the presence of chorionic villi and trophoblast cells. Laparoscopic surgery proved superior to open laparotomy due to its minimally invasive nature and faster recovery, with serial β-hCG measurements demonstrating favorable decline postoperatively. This case underscores the importance of timely diagnosis and prompt surgical intervention to prevent life-threatening complications such as massive hemorrhage and organ damage.

title: Hysterosalpingography and Endocervical Microbial Assessment of Infertile Women in Ogun State, Nigeria
summary:
# Summary

This hysterosalpingography and microbial assessment study identified tubal pathology as the leading anatomical abnormality in infertile Nigerian women, accounting for 36.1% of cases and primarily manifesting as tubal blockage (82.5%). Age, extended infertility duration, and genital microbes emerged as significant independent risk factors for tubal infertility, with women aged 30-39 years showing the highest prevalence of infertility and secondary infertility predominating at 77.8% of the cohort. Genital infections, particularly from sexually transmitted organisms like Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhea, contribute substantially to pelvic inflammatory disease and subsequent tubal disease through inflammatory and infectious mechanisms. Additionally, peritoneal factors including endometriosis, tuberculosis, and ectopic pregnancy history were identified as contributors to tubal subfertility, while unsafe abortion procedures significantly increased tubal and pelvic infection rates leading to secondary infertility. Microbiological analysis revealed Staphylococcus aureus as the predominant organism in high vaginal swabs, though Chlamydia species were notably absent, possibly due to widespread antibiotic use in urban settings.

title: Educational Case: Ectopic pregnancy and its relation to pelvic infections
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease, caused primarily by *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* and *Chlamydia trachomatis* infections, represents a significant risk factor for ectopic pregnancy through multiple mechanisms including loss of fallopian tube ciliation, scar tissue formation, and tubal structural damage. Additional risk factors for ectopic pregnancy include endometriosis, previous ectopic pregnancy, intrauterine device use, abdominal surgery, and advanced maternal age, with ruptured ectopic pregnancies carrying serious complications including severe bleeding and infection. Diagnosis relies on serum hCG measurement and ultrasound confirmation, while management options include medical treatment with methotrexate—which inhibits DNA synthesis and repair—or surgical interventions such as salpingostomy or salpingectomy depending on clinical stability. Pelvic inflammatory disease treatment utilizes antibiotic combinations including cefoxitin and doxycycline, with nucleic acid amplification testing recommended for identification of causative pathogens.

title: Positive Murphy's Sign of Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome
summary: Chlamydia trachomatis infection acquired through unprotected sexual intercourse causes pelvic inflammatory disease, which can progress to Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (FHCS), a rare complication characterized by perihepatic adhesions between the liver capsule and abdominal wall. Patients with FHCS present with right upper quadrant pain exacerbated by respiration as the liver moves against the inflamed peritoneum, along with lower abdominal pain and vaginal discharge typical of pelvic inflammatory disease. The condition can be clinically deceptive, as the right upper quadrant pain and positive Murphy's sign may mimic acute cholecystitis, though laboratory findings typically show normal liver enzymes with only slightly elevated C-reactive protein. Early antimicrobial therapy with ceftriaxone and minocycline hydrochloride is essential for treating FHCS and preventing serious complications including infertility and chronic perihepatic inflammation. A thorough clinical history including sexual history and pelvic symptoms is critical for distinguishing FHCS from hepatobiliary disease and ensuring appropriate diagnosis and treatment.

title: Pelvic Actinomycosis and Diagnostic Complexity: Case Report with Literature Review
summary: Pelvic actinomycosis is a rare infection caused by *Actinomyces israelii*, anaerobic bacteria normally found in the oral cavity, urogenital tract, and gastrointestinal tract that become pathogenic when mucosal barriers are compromised. Intrauterine device (IUD) insertion is a major risk factor, as it causes epithelial trauma and creates an environment favorable for bacterial proliferation, particularly when bacterial vaginosis reduces normal lactobacilli and increases vaginal pH. The infection characteristically produces suppurative granulomatous inflammation with tubo-ovarian abscesses and can present with nonspecific symptoms including pelvic pain, vaginal discharge or bleeding, fever, and systemic manifestations such as weight loss and night sweats. Diagnosis is definitively established through histopathological examination showing filamentous bacterial colonies with the characteristic Splendore-Hoeppli phenomenon, along with elevated inflammatory markers (elevated WBC, CRP, leukocytosis). Treatment traditionally involves prolonged antibiotic therapy with penicillin G as the first-line agent, with alternatives including clindamycin, macrolides, tetracycline, and doxycycline for penicillin-allergic patients, though severe cases with fistulas or obstructions may require surgical intervention such as hysterectomy or bowel resection.

title: Effect of Single and Multiple Methotrexate Doses Treatment on Ovarian Reserve in Ectopic Pregnancy Women: A Prospective Cohort Study
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

This prospective cohort study evaluated the safety of methotrexate (MTX) treatment on ovarian reserve in women with ectopic pregnancy, comparing single-dose versus multiple-dose regimens. Ovarian reserve was assessed using three key markers: follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), and antral follicle count (AFC), measured before MTX administration and eight weeks after treatment. The study found no significant differences in FSH, AMH, or AFC levels between the single-dose and multiple-dose MTX groups, indicating that both treatment regimens preserve ovarian reserve equally. While estradiol levels were statistically significantly elevated in the multiple-dose group, this finding did not translate to clinically meaningful impairment of ovarian function. These results demonstrate that MTX administration for ectopic pregnancy management, regardless of dosing strategy, does not negatively affect ovarian reserve and remains a safe therapeutic option for women of reproductive age.

title: Case report: Granulocytopenia induced by local injection of MTX for abdominal pregnancy
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare but serious adverse effect of intraoperative methotrexate injection for abdominal ectopic pregnancy management. While local methotrexate injection effectively treats persistent ectopic pregnancy by inducing trophoblast cell death and inhibiting placental villi regeneration, the patient in this case developed severe granulocytopenia and leukopenia postoperatively, with white blood cell counts dropping to 3.22 × 10⁹/L by postoperative day 4. The complication was successfully managed with recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and Di Yu Sheng Bai tablets. Although methotrexate is the first-line pharmacological treatment for ectopic pregnancy and local injection reduces the risk of persistent ectopic pregnancy after surgical extraction, clinicians should monitor hematologic parameters closely following this approach, particularly in patients with high-risk factors for persistent ectopic pregnancy, such as lesion size ≥2 cm and preoperative hCG levels ≥2500 IU/mL.

title: Impact of salpingectomy on the risk and characteristics of ectopic pregnancy after IVF/ICSI in patients with ectopic pregnancy history: A large retrospective cohort study
summary: This large retrospective cohort study examines ectopic pregnancy (EP) risk in IVF/ICSI patients with prior EP history and evaluates the protective role of salpingectomy. Patients with previous EP experience significantly elevated IVF-EP rates (2.2% for single prior EP, increasing with recurrent EP) compared to those without EP history (1.3%), with a 4.7-fold increased recurrence risk overall. While salpingectomy reduces tubal IVF-EP risk, bilateral salpingectomy paradoxically increases non-tubal IVF-EP rates and substantially compromises fertility outcomes by reducing implantation, biochemical pregnancy, clinical pregnancy, and live birth rates, likely through disruption of ovarian blood supply and follicular development. Multiple factors independently predict IVF-EP risk, including higher numbers of embryos transferred, previous EP episodes, younger maternal age, and reduced endometrial thickness, whereas blastocyst transfer reduces IVF-EP incidence in the general population but shows diminished protective effects in patients with recurrent EP. These findings suggest that while salpingectomy may selectively benefit patients with unilateral tubal disease, bilateral salpingectomy should be approached cautiously in EP-history patients given the shift toward non-tubal ectopic implantation and impaired reproductive outcomes.

title: Managing Pregnancies of Unknown Location With the M4 Prediction Model or the NICE Algorithm: A Randomised Controlled Trial With Cross‐Sectional Diagnostic Accuracy Data
summary: This randomized controlled trial compared the M4 prediction model with the NICE algorithm for managing pregnancies of unknown location (PUL), focusing on ectopic pregnancy detection and patient outcomes. The M4 model demonstrated non-inferior sensitivity for detecting ectopic pregnancies (79% versus NICE's 85%), but exhibited lower specificity for identifying non-ectopic pregnancies (67% versus 74%), resulting in a lower overall diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.80 versus 0.84). The M4 model facilitated more conservative management of ectopic pregnancies, with 63.4% managed without laparoscopic surgery compared to 44.3% in the NICE group, though this advantage was offset by increased false positives leading to prolonged diagnostic timelines and additional clinic visits. Serum hCG levels emerged as a critical variable affecting both algorithms' performance, with lower hCG concentrations impairing M4 sensitivity while higher levels correlated with treatment failure and longer hCG resolution times. The NICE algorithm demonstrated more consistent diagnostic performance across populations, and approximately 15% of protocol non-adherence was attributed to physician clinical judgment, highlighting that integrating clinician assessment with algorithmic guidance remains essential for safe PUL management.

title: Why should we check the tubes in IVF patients with ovarian endometriosis before embryo transfer? a retrospective study
summary: Ovarian endometriosis frequently causes hydrosalpinx (fluid-filled fallopian tubes), which occurs in approximately 8.5% of IVF patients with endometriomas and significantly compromises fertility outcomes. Hydrosalpinx reduces implantation and pregnancy rates while increasing the risk of ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage through multiple mechanisms, including direct toxic effects of tubal fluid on embryos and endometria, altered endometrial receptivity genes like HOXA10, and reduced anti-mullerian hormone levels. The study emphasizes that tubal assessment via hysterosalpingography and diagnostic laparoscopy is critical before embryo transfer in patients with ovarian endometriosis to identify occult hydrosalpinx. Surgical interventions including salpingectomy, tubal ligation, proximal tubal occlusion, or hydrosalpinx aspiration effectively treat hydrosalpinx and significantly improve pregnancy and live birth rates in IVF cycles. Additionally, ovarian endometriomas themselves reduce oocyte retrieval numbers and increase cycle cancellations while lowering anti-mullerian hormone levels, compounding fertility challenges in these patients. Therefore, comprehensive tubal evaluation and treatment before embryo transfer is essential to optimize IVF success in women with endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Heterotopic cervical pregnancy: Case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Cervical ectopic pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening condition associated with significant morbidity and mortality, characterized by implantation of the gestational sac in the cervix rather than the uterine cavity. Multiple risk factors contribute to cervical pregnancy development, including prior cesarean delivery, dilation and curettage procedures, pelvic inflammatory disease, assisted reproductive technologies, endometrial inflammation, and structural uterine abnormalities such as fibroids. Patients typically present with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain, and transvaginal ultrasonography is the primary diagnostic tool, with pelvic MRI providing supplementary diagnostic information. Conservative management involves intracervical injection of agents such as methotrexate, potassium chloride, or glucose, while surgical approaches including aspiration, curettage, hysteroscopy, or uterine artery embolization are often necessary to prevent complications including severe bleeding, hysterectomy, and maternal death. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment are critical to optimize maternal and fetal outcomes, and the condition remains inadequately characterized particularly in the context of assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Global burden of female infertility attributable to sexually transmitted infections and maternal sepsis: 1990–2021 and projections to 2050
summary:
# Summary

Sexually transmitted infections, particularly Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae, are major causes of female infertility globally, primarily through mechanisms of tubal factor infertility and pelvic inflammatory disease. These bacterial pathogens ascend through the female urogenital tract, triggering inflammation of oviduct epithelial surfaces that disrupts ovulation and leads to tubal damage, with chlamydial infection causing approximately 25% of pelvic inflammatory disease cases and accounting for 11-67% of infertility cases overall. Beyond sexually transmitted infections, maternal sepsis and other maternal infections contribute significantly to secondary infertility, with infection-related infertility burden showing clear age patterns, peaking in reproductive years and correlating inversely with socioeconomic development. Geographic disparities are pronounced, with Western Sub-Saharan Africa experiencing the highest prevalence rates and Tropical Latin America showing the fastest growth, while regions with low sociodemographic indices bear substantially greater burden. Risk factors for infection-related infertility include advanced age, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, intimate partner violence, and post-traumatic stress disorder, highlighting the intersection of biological and social determinants. Projections to 2050 indicate continued increases in chlamydial and other sexually transmitted infection-related infertility in younger women, emphasizing the urgent need for targeted prevention and treatment strategies.

title: A ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancy presenting as acute abdomen: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: Ovarian ectopic pregnancy represents a rare but life-threatening condition that occurs in highly vascularized ovarian tissue, causing potentially fatal intraperitoneal bleeding and hemorrhagic shock upon rupture. Multiple risk factors increase susceptibility, including intrauterine device use, assisted reproductive technologies, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and previous pelvic or abdominal surgeries, which collectively promote pelvic adhesions and disrupt normal ovum migration. The mechanisms facilitating ovarian implantation include fallopian tubal epithelial damage, retrograde embryo movement during embryo transfer, and direct ovarian trauma following oocyte retrieval. Clinically, patients present with the classic triad of abdominal pain, amenorrhea, and vaginal bleeding, progressing to hemorrhagic shock when rupture occurs. Diagnosis combines ultrasound imaging showing a cyst with wide echogenic outer ring and empty uterus, with definitive confirmation via histopathological examination revealing chorionic villi and trophoblastic cells within ovarian tissue using Spiegelberg's criteria. Surgical intervention via laparoscopic or open approaches remains the primary treatment modality, although methotrexate therapy shows promise in select cases.

title: Clinical applications, advances, and future directions in hysterosalpingography
summary:
# Summary

Tubal infertility accounts for approximately 12.8% of infertility cases and can result from multiple etiologies including pelvic inflammation, tuberculosis, septic abortion, and ectopic pregnancy. Hysterosalpingography (HSG) has traditionally served as the standard diagnostic method for evaluating fallopian tube patency and uterine morphology, though its use is declining due to ionizing radiation exposure. Contemporary ultrasound-based alternatives, particularly 4D-hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography (4D-HyCoSy), demonstrate high sensitivity (76-96%) and specificity for tubal patency assessment while avoiding radiation exposure, making them suitable for patients with radiation sensitivity or iodine allergies. Advanced HyCoSy techniques provide superior diagnostic accuracy and reduce operator dependence compared to traditional methods, and some patients achieve natural pregnancy following the diagnostic procedure itself. For severe hydrosalpinx cases unsuitable for surgical repair, laparoscopic salpingectomy prior to in vitro fertilization significantly improves implantation rates, while less severe cases may benefit from conservative approaches such as salpingostomy or expectant management when unilateral tubal patency is confirmed. Treatment selection should be individualized based on clinical complexity, patient resources, and reproductive goals.

title: MRI Revealing Ovarian Non-salvageable Torsion Masquerading as a Pelvic Mass in a Young Woman With Enterocystoplasty and Neurogenic Bladder
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian torsion is a gynecological emergency that causes irreversible ovarian damage through vascular compromise, presenting with sudden severe pelvic pain, nausea, and vomiting. Benign ovarian masses larger than 5 cm are the primary risk factor, with the right ovary affected more frequently due to stabilization by the sigmoid colon. Imaging diagnosis relies on ultrasound as the first-line modality, detecting characteristic findings including enlarged ovaries, peripheral follicle displacement, the whirlpool sign, and reduced or absent blood flow on Doppler; however, incomplete torsion can preserve arterial flow and cause false-negative results. MRI provides superior diagnostic accuracy, demonstrating ovarian stromal edema, hemorrhagic infarction, and lack of contrast enhancement indicating non-viable tissue, with diffusion-weighted imaging particularly valuable for assessing cytotoxic edema and distinguishing torsion from mimics such as hemorrhagic cysts, endometriomas, and ectopic pregnancy. This case illustrates the critical importance of prompt diagnosis, as the patient's infarcted ovary required oophorectomy due to irreversible ischemic damage, highlighting how delayed recognition of torsion masquerading as a pelvic mass can result in permanent loss of ovarian function.

title: Prevalence of Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and Ureaplasma urealyticum infections in males and females of childbearing age in Chengdu, China
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

This epidemiological study examined the prevalence of three sexually transmitted pathogens—Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and Ureaplasma urealyticum—in reproductive-age individuals in Chengdu, China, with implications for reproductive health complications. All three pathogens were found to cause significant urogenital pathology, with Chlamydia and Neisseria triggering inflammation that leads to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain, while Ureaplasma infection additionally associates with adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, low birth weight, and neonatal pneumonia. Mixed infections, particularly CT+NG combinations, exacerbate inflammatory responses beyond single infections. Overall pathogen prevalence was 27.8%, with notably higher rates in females and individuals aged 18-24, a pattern particularly pronounced for Ureaplasma (49.29% in females aged 18-24) and Chlamydia. Females demonstrated significantly elevated rates of both single and mixed infections compared to males, with mixed infections most prevalent in the urogenital inflammation diagnostic group, underscoring the clinical burden of these infections in women of childbearing potential.

title: Isolated Torsion of the Fallopian Tube: A Novel Presentation
summary: Isolated fallopian tube torsion (IFTT) is a rare cause of acute pelvic pain with diverse etiologies categorized as either intrinsic factors—including congenital malformations, hydrosalpinx, pelvic inflammatory disease, and tubal ligation—or extrinsic factors such as adhesions, endometriosis, tumors, ectopic pregnancy, and trauma. The condition presents with acute pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, and systemic signs including fever and leukocytosis, with hematosalpinx causing ischemic changes in the affected fallopian tube. While ultrasound and CT imaging provide initial diagnostic support, laparoscopy remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis and management, with salpingectomy serving as the primary therapeutic intervention. Clinically, IFTT must be differentiated from other acute pelvic conditions including ovarian torsion, ectopic pregnancy, hemorrhagic ovarian cysts, appendicitis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis-related acute pain. The recognition of IFTT as a distinct clinical entity is important for accurate diagnosis and appropriate surgical management in patients presenting with acute pelvic symptoms.

title: A Model for Predicting Severe Intra-Abdominal Adhesions following Prior Cesarean Sections
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a predictive model for severe intra-abdominal adhesions following cesarean sections, a leading cause of adhesion formation during childbearing years. The research identified multiple significant risk factors for severe post-cesarean adhesions, including the number of prior cesarean sections (particularly two or more), elevated maternal body mass index, maternal morbidity at the time of previous surgery, and prolonged operative time. Severe adhesions carry substantial clinical consequences, including increased risk of bowel obstruction, urinary tract injury, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain in subsequent pregnancies. The study found that peritoneal and rectus muscle closure may provide preventive benefit against post-cesarean adhesion formation. These findings support the development of clinical prediction tools to identify high-risk patients and inform surgical strategies to minimize adhesion severity and reduce downstream gynecological and obstetrical complications.

title: Maternal plasma microRNAs as potential biomarkers for triaging pregnancies of unknown location and ectopic pregnancy diagnosis
summary: Maternal plasma microRNAs show promise as biomarkers for distinguishing ectopic pregnancy from viable intrauterine pregnancy in cases of unknown location. Multiple microRNAs are differentially expressed in ectopic pregnancies, with hsa-miR-21-5p significantly elevated (2.8-fold higher) and hsa-miR-411-5p reduced, while additional miRNAs including hsa-miR-1246, hsa-miR-222-3p, and hsa-miR-30d-5p are also substantially elevated in ectopic cases. Using a combined ratio of hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-411-5p improved diagnostic accuracy substantially (ROC AUC = 0.74), demonstrating superior discrimination compared to individual markers. These microRNA signatures are detectable as early as four gestational weeks and likely reflect dysregulation of implantation, trophoblast invasion, and blastocyst development processes. The study identifies overlapping microRNA patterns associated with miscarriage risk, including hsa-miR-1246, hsa-miR-130a-3p, and hsa-miR-30d-5p, suggesting these markers may help triage pregnancies of unknown location by simultaneously assessing viability and location.

title: Treatment of Cesarean Scar Ectopic Pregnancy with Hysteroscopic Resection
summary: Cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) is a serious ectopic pregnancy complication arising from previous cesarean delivery, associated with life-threatening conditions including placenta accreta spectrum, fetal death, preterm birth, and hemorrhagic morbidity that accounts for 9-14% of first-trimester maternal deaths. The condition is diagnosed primarily through transvaginal ultrasonography, supplemented by Doppler ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, and hysteroscopy, with pathophysiology involving fetal cell implantation through microscopic scar defects and altered endometrial leukocyte counts at the cesarean scar site. Hysteroscopic resection emerges as the preferred minimally invasive treatment approach, effectively reducing beta-hCG titers, preserving fertility, and restoring normal menstrual cycles, though it carries risks of infection, bleeding, and uterine rupture. Alternative or adjunctive treatments include systemic and local methotrexate therapy (particularly effective when preoperative beta-hCG exceeds 10,000 mIU/mL), laparoscopic surgery, uterine artery embolization, and intrauterine injections of potassium chloride or vasopressin, with more invasive options like laparotomy and hysterectomy reserved for cases requiring definitive management. This study demonstrates that hysteroscopic resection is a valuable fertility-preserving strategy for CSP management, though clinicians must monitor for potential complications such as intrauterine adhesions that may develop post-treatment.

title: Diagnostic Value of Physical Examination, Ultrasound, and Radiography Compared to Computed Tomography in the Evaluation of Nontraumatic Left Lower Quadrant Acute Abdominal Pain
summary:
# Summary

Left lower quadrant pain presents a diagnostic challenge in acute abdominal emergencies, with a broad differential diagnosis including gastrointestinal conditions (colitis, diverticulitis, inflammatory bowel disease), genitourinary disorders (pyelonephritis, renal stones), gynecological emergencies (ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion, endometriosis), and vascular complications (aneurysm, dissection). This study compared the diagnostic performance of physical examination, plain abdominal radiography (PAR), and ultrasonography (US) for evaluating nontraumatic left lower quadrant acute abdominal pain. Physical examination demonstrated the highest sensitivity (75.47%) but poor specificity (16.77%), while ultrasonography achieved superior specificity and negative predictive value with greater overall diagnostic accuracy than PAR. Plain abdominal radiography showed the lowest diagnostic accuracy (35.91%) among the three modalities. The findings indicate that ultrasonography is the most reliable imaging modality for confirming diagnoses in acute left lower quadrant pain, while physical examination remains valuable for initial assessment to narrow the differential diagnosis.

title: Recurrent Tubo-Ovarian Abscesses in a Non-sexually Active Adolescent: A Case Report and Review of Atypical Risk Factors
summary:
# Summary

Tubo-ovarian abscesses (TOAs) are severe suppurative complications of pelvic inflammatory disease classically caused by ascending infections from sexually transmitted pathogens such as Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. This case report documents recurrent TOA in a non-sexually active adolescent, highlighting atypical risk factors including obesity, recurrent urinary tract infections, poor perineal hygiene, congenital genitourinary anomalies, and Crohn's disease—conditions that can predispose to ascending infections independent of sexual transmission. The patient presented with fever, abdominal pain, and leukocytosis, with elevated inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein and cancer antigen-125, and imaging revealed Escherichia coli cultured from urine, suggesting an enteric source of infection. Treatment with broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics (cefoxitin, doxycycline, and metronidazole) combined with ultrasound-guided percutaneous drainage successfully prevented life-threatening complications such as abscess rupture, peritonitis, and sepsis. This case emphasizes that TOAs can develop through non-traditional pathogenic mechanisms in sexually inactive individuals and demonstrates that prompt multidisciplinary intervention with imaging-guided drainage and antibiotics is essential for favorable outcomes.

title: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy: From Diagnosis to Treatment
summary: Tubal ectopic pregnancy, the most common form of ectopic pregnancy occurring in approximately 95% of cases, represents a serious reproductive complication that can progress to tubal rupture and life-threatening hemorrhage. Major risk factors include prior ectopic pregnancy, tubal pathology, assisted reproductive technologies, pelvic inflammatory disease from Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection, endometriosis, smoking, and advanced maternal age. Diagnosis relies on a combination of serial β-hCG measurements, transvaginal ultrasonography, and laparoscopy, while management depends on clinical stability, with methotrexate as the preferred medical option for hemodynamically stable patients with low β-hCG levels and surgical interventions (salpingectomy or salpingostomy) for unstable or ruptured cases. The pathophysiology involves impaired embryonic hormonal signaling, altered uterine peristalsis, and fallopian tube transport irregularities that direct the embryo away from the uterine cavity, with endometriosis particularly damaging tubal function through inflammatory mechanisms. Long-term consequences of ectopic pregnancy include infertility, recurrence risk, and psychological distress, necessitating comprehensive management approaches that address both acute medical emergencies and subsequent reproductive health outcomes.

title: Predictors of Methotrexate Success and Fertility Outcomes in Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy: A Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary: Methotrexate Success and Fertility Outcomes in Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy

Ectopic pregnancy is a life-threatening condition with multiple risk factors including pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, intrauterine device use, and prior ectopic pregnancy, making early diagnosis via transvaginal ultrasound and β-hCG measurement critical to prevent severe complications such as tubal rupture and hemoperitoneum. Methotrexate has become an effective medical treatment for hemodynamically stable patients, with approximately 72% achieving resolution of ectopic pregnancy, though gestational sac diameter emerges as the most reliable predictor of treatment success—sacs larger than 20 mm carry significantly higher failure risk and are associated with the need for surgical intervention via salpingectomy or salpingostomy. Logistic regression analysis identified gestational sac diameter as the only factor significantly associated with methotrexate failure, whereas fetal pole presence and elevated β-hCG levels also increased failure risk, though β-hCG level and gestational age alone were not significant predictors. Both medical and surgical management approaches yielded comparable reproductive outcomes, with 68% of patients achieving term live birth regardless of treatment modality, and notably, methotrexate-treated patients showed higher rates of spontaneous subsequent pregnancy. Recent clinical practice demonstrates a progressive shift toward conservative methotrexate management from 2019 to 2023, reflecting growing recognition of its safety and efficacy in preserving fertility while avoiding surgical complications such as persistent trophoblastic disease associated with salpingostomy.

title: Intra-abdominal hemorrhage following ultrasound-guided transvaginal oocyte retrieval: Retrospective analysis of 25 cases
summary:
# Summary

Intra-abdominal hemorrhage (IAH) is a rare but potentially life-threatening complication of ultrasound-guided transvaginal oocyte retrieval occurring in 0.06–0.27% of cases, where the aspiration needle penetrates the vaginal wall and ovarian surface, potentially damaging vascular networks. Pelvic adhesions, particularly those associated with endometriosis, prior oocyte pick-up procedures, or previous pelvic surgery, significantly increase the risk of hemorrhage. The condition typically manifests within 12 hours post-procedure with abdominal pain, distension, dizziness, and hemodynamic changes reflected by hemoglobin and hematocrit reductions averaging 26.5 mg/dL and 7.7%, respectively. Management approaches included conservative treatment with bed rest, hemostatic agents (such as reptilase), and fluid resuscitation in 17 patients, while 8 patients with severe hemorrhage and progressive hemoglobin decline or hemodynamic instability required surgical intervention with laparoscopy or laparotomy for hemostasis. Despite the severity of this complication, reproductive outcomes were not compromised: 52% of patients achieved live births with no increased risk of miscarriage or ectopic pregnancy, and both conservative and surgical management approaches yielded similar clinical pregnancy rates of approximately 50–53%.

title: Recurrent Pelvic Inflammatory Disease After Serial Intrauterine Inseminations
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This case report documents recurrent pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) following serial intrauterine inseminations (IUI), highlighting a serious but underrecognized complication of fertility treatment. IUI causes PID through multiple mechanisms: the procedure bypasses the protective cervical mucosal barrier, facilitating migration of vaginal microorganisms into the sterile uterine cavity, while the timing of IUI coincides with natural disruption of the ovarian outer layer during ovulation and enhanced uterine contractility that further disseminates pathogens. Risk factors for post-IUI PID include multiple sexual partners, prior PID history, surgical procedures on the upper genital tract, and use of husband sperm rather than donor sperm, with reported incidence ranging from 0 to 0.21 per 1000 IUI cycles. PID results in severe complications including deterioration of fertility and permanent adnexal damage, and is most commonly caused by mixed vaginal microflora, though sexually transmitted infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and Mycoplasma genitalium are also frequently implicated. The clinical case responded to combination therapy consisting of intravenous ampicillin plus sulbactam followed by oral doxycycline, along with surgical intervention, which led to rapid clinical improvement and resolution of elevated inflammatory markers. This case underscores the importance of pre-IUI screening, stringent aseptic technique, and consideration of underlying anatomical or microbiological factors in patients experiencing recurrent post-IUI PID.

title: Interpregnancy interval and risk of recurrence following tubal ectopic pregnancy: retrospective cohort study from UK tertiary center
summary: This retrospective cohort study from a UK tertiary center examined factors influencing recurrence risk after tubal ectopic pregnancy (TEP), finding that interpregnancy interval timing critically affects outcomes. Women conceiving within 6 months of TEP had the lowest recurrence risk, while intervals of 6-18 months and >18 months were associated with four-fold and five-fold increased odds of recurrence, respectively, likely reflecting the time required for tubal injury resolution. A history of multiple previous TEPs (≥2) and presence of a live embryo in the index pregnancy also significantly increased recurrence risk, suggesting underlying tubal pathology. Notably, expectant management was associated with similar recurrence rates to surgical management, supporting it as a safe, cost-effective alternative when clinically appropriate, though women with multiple prior TEPs may benefit from salpingectomy given contralateral tube involvement in most recurrences. The study identifies key patient counseling points: rapid conception is protective, nulliparity and prior pelvic inflammatory disease increase baseline risk, and surgical vs. conservative approaches show comparable outcomes, allowing individualized treatment planning.

title: Multimodality imaging techniques of diaphragmatic ectopic pregnancy: a case report and review of the literature
summary: This case report documents a rare diaphragmatic ectopic pregnancy, an unusual abdominal implantation site distinct from the more common tubal pregnancies that account for 95% of ectopic cases. Ectopic pregnancies pose substantial clinical risk, causing intraperitoneal bleeding and high mortality rates due to diagnostic challenges, with risk factors including tubal infections, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, smoking, and assisted reproductive procedures like in vitro fertilization and curettage. Abdominal ectopic pregnancies typically implant in the Douglas pouch, omentum, or solid organs including liver and spleen, though diaphragmatic implantation remains exceptionally rare and carries particular danger due to limited vascularization that promotes early rupture and hemorrhage. Clinical presentation involves nonspecific symptoms such as abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding, with elevated human chorionic gonadotropin levels providing diagnostic support. Multimodality imaging including ultrasonography, computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and specialized techniques like diffusion-weighted imaging and fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography/computed tomography enables accurate localization of ectopic gestational sacs. Treatment options encompass both surgical approaches (laparoscopy or laparotomy) and pharmacological intervention with methotrexate or potassium chloride, with surgical management being essential in cases with severe hemorrhagic complications.

title: Coexisting tubal pregnancy and intrauterine pregnancy during a natural pregnancy, first diagnosed via ultrasonography: A case report
summary: This case report describes heterotopic pregnancy (HP), the rare coexistence of intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies, which occurred naturally in a nulliparous woman and was diagnosed via transvaginal ultrasonography. While HP is traditionally associated with assisted reproductive technology, this case demonstrates that it can occur during natural pregnancy, highlighting the diagnostic challenge of detecting ectopic pregnancy when an intrauterine pregnancy masks its presence. The fallopian tube is the most common implantation site for ectopic pregnancies in HP, and rupture carries significant risk due to abundant local vascularization and trophoblastic invasion, potentially causing life-threatening hemorrhage, abdominal pain, and peritonitis. Risk factors for HP overlap substantially with those for ectopic pregnancy alone, including smoking, pelvic inflammatory disease, prior tubal surgery, endometriosis, and ovarian stimulation. The primary treatment involves surgical resection of the ectopic pregnancy while preserving the intrauterine pregnancy, as demonstrated by left fallopian tube salpingectomy in this case, with progesterone support maintained for the viable intrauterine pregnancy. This case underscores the importance of vigilant ultrasound screening and awareness that elevated β-hCG levels and normal progression do not exclude coexisting ectopic pregnancy.

title: Case Report: Heterotopic pregnancy after adenomyosis surgery: a rare case highlighting diagnostic pitfalls and clinical insights
summary: This case report describes a rare heterotopic pregnancy (simultaneous intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy) in a patient with adenomyosis who had undergone multiple uterine surgical interventions including laparoscopic adenomyomectomy, myomectomy, and bilateral salpingectomy. The patient's adenomyosis, combined with surgical-induced myometrial defects, created structural vulnerability that facilitated ectopic implantation despite previous tubal ligation, demonstrating how compromised endometrial integrity and weakened uterine walls increase ectopic pregnancy risk. Key risk factors for both heterotopic and ectopic pregnancy include prior uterine interventions, adenomyosis, endometriosis, assisted reproductive technology, and pelvic inflammatory disease, with adenomyosis uniquely associated with implantation failure and intramural pregnancy development. Diagnostic challenges arose because common symptoms of abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding overlap between normal and ectopic pregnancies, highlighting the critical importance of ultrasound and MRI imaging to identify ectopic implantation sites within the myometrium. Management of heterotopic pregnancy differs fundamentally from standard ectopic pregnancy because preserving the intrauterine pregnancy is paramount, contraindicating systemic methotrexate; instead, local injection therapies with potassium chloride or hypertonic glucose, combined with surgical intervention when necessary, protect the viable intrauterine fetus. This case underscores that adenomyosis surgery, while alleviating symptoms, paradoxically increases ectopic pregnancy risk through myometrial damage, requiring heightened clinical vigilance during subsequent fertility treatment.

title: Abdominal Pregnancy: Challenges in Recognition and Diagnosis in the Emergency Department
summary:
# Abdominal Pregnancy: Recognition and Clinical Implications

Abdominal pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening form of ectopic pregnancy with extremely high maternal mortality (up to 20%) and fetal mortality (over 90%), presenting with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, anemia, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Risk factors overlap substantially with other ectopic pregnancies and include pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, assisted reproductive technology use, prior tubal surgery, and delayed motherhood. Abdominal pregnancies can generate severe complications ranging from local infections (fistula, abscess) to systemic manifestations including bowel obstruction, sepsis, renal failure, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Diagnostic imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, abdominal ultrasound, CT, and MRI (which is safe for both mother and fetus) are essential for confirmation, though diagnosis remains challenging in emergency settings. Definitive treatment requires exploratory laparotomy with removal of fetus and placenta, sometimes necessitating additional procedures such as salpingo-oophorectomy, while survivors often experience birth defects and post-natal complications. Early patient education, improved compliance across diverse populations, and increased clinical suspicion are critical for reducing the burden of this rare but catastrophic condition, particularly in low- and middle-income countries.

title: The unfortunate twin: A case report of intrapartum diagnosis of heterotopic pregnancy
summary: Heterotopic pregnancy, the simultaneous occurrence of intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies, is a rare condition with an incidence of approximately 1 in 30,000 natural conceptions but significantly higher rates following assisted reproductive technology (ART). Risk factors for heterotopic pregnancy overlap substantially with those for ectopic pregnancy and include previous ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, smoking, tubal pathology, and intrauterine device use. Clinical presentation involves lower abdominal pain, amenorrhea, and vaginal spotting, while rupture of the ectopic component leads to hemoperitoneum with potentially life-threatening complications including hemorrhagic shock and syncope. Transvaginal ultrasound serves as the first-line diagnostic tool, with MRI and laparoscopy providing additional diagnostic support when uncertainty remains. Surgical management through salpingectomy, salpingostomy, or oophorectomy remains the gold standard for treating the ectopic component, while methotrexate is contraindicated in this context; conservative approaches using potassium chloride or hyperosmolar glucose injection may be considered to minimize fetal complications from anesthesia.

title: Secondary mid-term abdominal pregnancy: A case report
summary: This case report documents a secondary abdominal pregnancy associated with endometriosis, presenting as a rare ectopic condition where the gestational sac implants in the peritoneal cavity rather than the fallopian tube. The patient presented with classic symptoms including nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and vaginal bleeding, with complications including fallopian tube rupture and intraperitoneal bleeding. Diagnosis was confirmed through multiple imaging modalities including ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, and computed tomography, which revealed placental attachment to the omentum and left fallopian tube. While conservative treatments such as methotrexate, potassium chloride, and mifepristone exist, surgical intervention—either laparoscopic or open cesarean section—remains the most effective treatment approach, with additional techniques like placental vascular embolization employed to minimize intraoperative hemorrhage. The case highlights the association between abdominal pregnancy and endometriosis, and underscores the importance of comprehensive imaging evaluation and surgical management for this rare but potentially life-threatening complication.

title: Diagnosing and Managing Trophoblastic Lesions in Cesarean Scars: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Gestational trophoblastic disease originates in the placenta and is characterized by abnormal trophoblast proliferation, with gestational trophoblastic neoplasia representing the invasive and metastatic forms that can originate from cesarean scar pregnancies following defective myometrial and endometrial healing. Cesarean scar trophoblastic lesions present clinically with vaginal bleeding, amenorrhea, and pelvic pain, and are most frequently hydatidiform moles, followed by epithelioid trophoblastic tumors and choriocarcinoma, with diagnostic differentiation aided by serum β-hCG levels that are elevated in choriocarcinoma but normal or low in other lesion types. Diagnosis relies on a multimodal imaging approach combining transvaginal ultrasonography with color Doppler imaging and magnetic resonance imaging to assess myometrial invasion, with histopathology providing definitive confirmation. Treatment strategies are primarily surgical, including suction curettage, hysterectomy, and minimally invasive laparoscopic or hysteroscopic resection for fertility preservation, while choriocarcinoma and other high-risk neoplasias require adjunctive systemic chemotherapy with methotrexate-based regimens or EMA-CO. Early diagnosis and intervention are critical to prevent metastasis and improve outcomes in this rare but serious complication of cesarean delivery.

title: Isthmic tubal ectopic pregnancy from a partial molar pregnancy: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of isthmic tubal ectopic pregnancy arising from a partial hydatidiform mole, a condition clinically indistinguishable from nonmolar ectopic pregnancy but requiring different management. Partial hydatidiform moles result from fertilization of a haploid ovum by two sperm or a diploid sperm and carry a 0.5–6% risk of malignant gestational trophoblastic neoplasia, substantially lower than the 15–20% risk associated with complete moles. Diagnosis requires histopathological examination supported by ancillary techniques including p57 immunohistochemical staining (which is expressed in partial moles but absent in complete moles), flow cytometry, and molecular analyses. Elevated β-hCG levels are monitored weekly until undetectable and then monthly for one month post-diagnosis to exclude malignant transformation, and patients require contraception during this monitoring period, with methotrexate treatment employed in approximately 28% of cases when gestational trophoblastic neoplasia develops. Preoperative imaging with magnetic resonance imaging may help identify molar tissue as heterogeneously enhancing cystic lesions in hemodynamically stable patients, though ultrasonography lacks sufficient discriminatory power to distinguish tubal molar pregnancies from nonmolar ectopic pregnancies.

title: Live normally sited pregnancy with concurrent residual tubal ectopic pregnancy: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a rare clinical scenario of concurrent intrauterine and residual tubal ectopic pregnancy. Ectopic pregnancies occur most commonly in the fallopian tubes and can be managed through expectant observation, medical therapy with methotrexate, or surgical intervention via salpingectomy or salpingotomy. Transvaginal ultrasound serves as the primary diagnostic imaging modality, while serial β-hCG monitoring is essential for assessing treatment success, with biochemical resolution defined as β-hCG levels below 20 IU/L. Residual ectopic pregnancies pose significant clinical risks including tubal rupture, hemorrhage, abdominal pain, abnormal bleeding, and amenorrhea, often necessitating surgical management. This unusual presentation highlights the importance of careful radiological follow-up and vigilant clinical monitoring to detect residual ectopic tissue, particularly in patients managed expectantly, to prevent potentially life-threatening complications.

title: Case Report: Multidisciplinary management of primary omental pregnancy involving intraoperative ultrasound
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a rare instance of omental pregnancy, a type of abdominal pregnancy where a gestational sac implants in the omentum rather than the uterus, representing a life-threatening condition with high risk of maternal mortality from hemorrhage, shock, and organ failure. The patient presented with elevated human chorionic gonadotropin levels (69,305 mIU/L), reduced hemoglobin, and elevated white blood cell count, with chronic pelvic inflammatory disease identified as a key risk factor that alters the fallopian tube microenvironment and promotes abnormal implantation through adhesion formation. Diagnostic imaging proved critical, with transabdominal sonography successfully identifying the gestational sac in the abdominal cavity after transvaginal sonography ruled out intrauterine pregnancy, while intraoperative ultrasonography further guided surgical localization. The multidisciplinary management approach combined laparoscopic surgical resection of the omental pregnancy with supportive care including iron sucrose for anemia treatment and cefuroxime for infection prevention. This case highlights the importance of recognizing risk factors such as pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and previous tubal surgery, and demonstrates that intraoperative ultrasound significantly enhances surgical precision in managing this rare but dangerous ectopic pregnancy variant.

title: Impact of Antibiotic Stewardship on the Management of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: A Multicenter Gynecological and Public Health Perspective in Pakistan
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), commonly caused by *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* and *Chlamydia trachomatis*, leads to serious complications including infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and persistent pelvic discomfort if left untreated. Clinical presentation typically includes lower abdominal discomfort, abnormal vaginal discharge, fever, dyspareunia, and irregular bleeding. This multicenter study in Pakistan demonstrated that implementing structured Antibiotic Stewardship Programs (ASPs) significantly improves PID management by enhancing guideline adherence (56.84% to 84.50%), increasing complete clinical recovery rates (62.63% to 79.00%), and reducing hospital admissions (38.42% to 24.50%), treatment duration (11.6 to 9.2 days), and symptom recurrence. ASPs achieved these improvements by optimizing appropriate antimicrobial use and reducing antibiotic resistance-related referrals by 57.69%, countering the detrimental effects of antimicrobial resistance and antibiotic overuse on treatment efficacy and healthcare costs.

title: Chlamydia trachomatis-Specific Antibodies and In Vitro Fertilization Outcome
summary: Chlamydia trachomatis infection significantly compromises female fertility through multiple mechanisms, including pelvic inflammatory disease and tubal factor infertility, both of which increase the risk of ectopic pregnancy. This study examined whether chlamydial antibodies, particularly anti-MOMP IgG and IgA, serve as markers of infection severity and predictors of IVF outcome. Women positive for anti-MOMP antibodies in follicular fluid showed substantially reduced implantation rates, lower oocyte maturation, and approximately half the pregnancy success rates compared to antibody-negative women, suggesting local immune responses within the reproductive tract actively impair embryo development. Advanced maternal age emerged as an independent negative factor, reducing odds of biochemical pregnancy, implantation, and live birth. The findings support serological screening for chlamydial antibodies as a cost-effective pre-IVF diagnostic tool to identify women at high risk of implantation failure, while the presence of anti-MOMP antibodies in follicular fluid indicates persistent or active infection warranting further clinical intervention before assisted reproduction.

title: Cross-Sectional Imaging of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Diagnostic Pearls and Pitfalls on CT and MR
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is primarily caused by sexually transmitted infections, particularly *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, with approximately 15% of untreated chlamydial infections progressing to PID. When infection ascends from the lower genital tract, it triggers a cascade of complications including endometritis, salpingitis, tubo-ovarian abscess, and peritonitis, with salpingitis being the earliest and most common manifestation. Untreated or delayed diagnosis of PID leads to serious long-term sequelae including infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and ectopic pregnancy, with salpingitis alone responsible for tubal damage that predisposes to these adverse outcomes. Advanced imaging, particularly MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), achieves superior diagnostic accuracy (98% sensitivity) compared to standard MRI (90.7%), and is especially useful for detecting purulent complications like pyosalpinx and differentiating PID from endometriosis. Computed tomography remains valuable in emergency settings for detecting acute complications including tubo-ovarian abscess, peritonitis, and Fitz-Hugh–Curtis syndrome, while transvaginal ultrasound serves as the first-line imaging modality for suspected PID evaluation.

title: A Partial Hydatidiform Mole in an Ovarian Ectopic Pregnancy: An Exceptional Occurrence
summary: This article reports an exceptionally rare case of a partial hydatidiform mole (PHM) occurring in an ovarian ectopic pregnancy (OEP), combining two unusual pathological conditions. Ovarian ectopic pregnancies are associated with multiple risk factors including intrauterine device use, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and in vitro fertilization, with patients typically presenting with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and elevated beta-hCG levels. The diagnosis of PHM was confirmed through immunohistochemistry showing p57kip2 nuclear positivity in villous cytotrophoblast and short tandem repeat analysis revealing a triploid 69,XXX karyotype, which distinguishes PHM from complete hydatidiform moles that lack p57kip2 expression in this location. Partial hydatidiform moles characteristically contain evidence of fetal development and arise from fertilization of a haploid ovum by two sperm or one diploid sperm, resulting in two paternal and one maternal haploid genome. While PHMs carry a lower risk of progressing to gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (approximately 1.5%) compared to complete moles (15-20%), strict clinical follow-up with beta-hCG monitoring was implemented to detect any malignant transformation. This case underscores the diagnostic importance of ancillary molecular techniques and the need for heightened clinical vigilance in managing atypical ectopic pregnancies.

title: Heterotopic pregnancy: a case report about triplets
summary:
# Summary

Heterotopic pregnancy, defined as the simultaneous presence of an intrauterine pregnancy and an ectopic pregnancy, is a rare but serious condition more frequently associated with medically assisted procreation than natural conception. This case report describes a 29-year-old woman who presented with pelvic pain and metrorrhagia following ovarian induction, with markedly elevated beta-hCG levels (>225,000 mIU/ml) and imaging findings of enlarged, hyperstimulated ovaries. Although pelvic ultrasound alone proved insufficient for diagnosis, supplemental pelvic MRI confirmed both an intrauterine triplet pregnancy and an abdominal ectopic gestational sac, along with haemoperitoneum indicating rupture. Abdominal ectopic pregnancies are particularly dangerous, accounting for only 1.4% of ectopic pregnancies but carrying substantially elevated morbidity and mortality risk due to trophoblast invasion of maternal tissues and massive hemorrhage potential. The patient underwent emergency laparotomy for surgical management of the ectopic pregnancy while preserving the intrauterine triplet pregnancy, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis through comprehensive imaging in medically assisted procreation cases to enable timely intervention and preserve fertility outcomes.

title: The efficacy of single-incision laparoscopic surgery versus conventional laparoscopic surgery in the surgical management of ectopic pregnancy: A systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, occurring in approximately 1–2% of pregnancies, remains a leading cause of maternal mortality and can present with severe complications including massive hemoperitoneum and hemorrhagic shock. This meta-analysis compared single-incision laparoscopic surgery (SILS) with conventional laparoscopic surgery (CLS) for surgical management of ectopic pregnancy, both established alternatives to open laparotomy. The analysis found that SILS significantly reduced blood loss (by 51 mL), shortened postoperative hospital stay by 0.24 days, and accelerated return of bowel function compared to CLS, while achieving superior cosmetic outcomes with 46.2% of SILS patients reporting high satisfaction with wound appearance versus 19.3% in the CLS group. However, SILS presented technical challenges including impaired visualization, instrument interference, and loss of laparoscopic triangulation that did not translate into significantly longer operative times. The authors conclude that SILS represents a viable alternative to CLS for appropriately selected patients, particularly those with tubal ectopic pregnancies and minimal hemoperitoneum, though patient selection, surgeon experience, and specialized equipment costs warrant consideration in clinical practice.

title: Ectopic pregnancies: laparoscopic versus vNOTES approach. Surgical and obstetric outcomes
summary: This study compares vNOTES (vaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery) with traditional laparoscopy for treating ectopic pregnancies, which most commonly occur in the fallopian tubes and represent a significant clinical challenge. Both approaches effectively treat ectopic pregnancy through salpingectomy or salpingostomy, with comparable safety profiles including low complication and infection rates. The vNOTES technique demonstrated significant advantages by reducing surgical time and postoperative pain while avoiding abdominal wall scarring through its fully vaginal approach, though it is contraindicated in patients with endometriosis due to anatomical concerns. Importantly, vNOTES salpingectomy did not increase risks of adverse obstetric outcomes including miscarriage, perineal tears, or cesarean delivery in subsequent pregnancies, nor did it increase dyspareunia or vaginal complications. Multiple established risk factors for ectopic pregnancy were identified including prior ectopic pregnancies, pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal surgeries, cigarette smoking, intrauterine device use, advanced maternal age, and assisted reproductive technologies. Overall, vNOTES represents a safe and effective minimally invasive alternative to laparoscopy for ectopic pregnancy management with superior perioperative outcomes.

title: Chinese herbal medicine in the treatment of ectopic pregnancy
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Ectopic pregnancy represents a life-threatening condition and is the leading cause of pregnancy-related deaths in the first trimester. Chinese herbal medicine (CHM) has been traditionally used in Chinese hospitals to treat ectopic pregnancy, demonstrating clinical utility in this context. The combination of CHM with Western medicine produces superior outcomes compared to either treatment alone, including significantly higher treatment success rates and faster resolution of abdominal pain. Additionally, the combined approach of CHM plus Western medicine more effectively reduces beta-hCG elevation compared to Western medicine monotherapy, suggesting complementary mechanisms of action. These findings support the potential value of integrating traditional Chinese herbal approaches with conventional Western medical interventions for managing ectopic pregnancy, though further high-quality evidence is needed to optimize treatment protocols.

title: A Trio of Atypical Ectopic Pregnancies
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: This case series examines three atypical ectopic pregnancies and their management, highlighting the serious clinical consequences of ectopic implantation. Ectopic pregnancies are potentially life-threatening conditions presenting with pelvic pain and abnormal uterine bleeding that can cause maternal morbidity and mortality. The cases demonstrate the association between endometriosis and fallopian tube pathology, as endometriosis was found in the right fallopian tube and omentum, sites where ectopic implantation occurred. Notably, heterotopic pregnancies—where both intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies coexist—are rare events occurring in approximately 1 in 30,000 spontaneous pregnancies, with spontaneous abortion occurring in 26% of these cases. Laparoscopic and robotic surgical interventions including salpingectomy, omentectomy, and evacuation of hemoperitoneum were employed to manage the ectopic pregnancies and their complications. The findings underscore the importance of thorough abdominopelvic surveys during surgical evaluation to identify endometriosis and other anatomical abnormalities that may predispose to ectopic pregnancy.

title: Spontaneous rupture of bicornuate uterus in second trimester: A case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare instance of second-trimester spontaneous uterine rupture in a patient with bicornuate uterus, a congenital anomaly resulting from incomplete fusion of the Mullerian ducts during embryonic development. Bicornuate uterus is a significant risk factor for uterine rupture and is associated with multiple reproductive complications including infertility, endometriosis, spontaneous abortion, preterm delivery, and cervical insufficiency. The patient presented with classic signs of uterine rupture including severe abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, hypotension, and tachycardia, with imaging revealing hemoperitoneum requiring emergency intervention. While uterine rupture is typically associated with obstetric factors such as prior cesarean section, advanced maternal age, placenta increta, and fetal macrosomia, this case demonstrates that congenital uterine anomalies alone can precipitate rupture even without these conventional risk factors. The report emphasizes that bicornuate uterus should be recognized as an independent risk factor for spontaneous uterine rupture during pregnancy, requiring heightened clinical vigilance in patients with known uterine malformations.

title: Retroperitoneal ectopic pregnancy localized to the mesorectum: A case report and mini-review of literature
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

Retroperitoneal ectopic pregnancy (REP) is an exceptionally rare and life-threatening form of ectopic pregnancy that frequently goes undiagnosed due to its atypical anatomical location and nonspecific clinical presentation, such as abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. This case report describes a patient with mesorectal ectopic pregnancy—implantation in the retroperitoneal space near the rectum—where the pregnancy mass was initially missed during transvaginal ultrasound and laparoscopy, leading to delayed diagnosis. Imaging with abdominal computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging proved critical for identifying the retroperitoneal gestational mass, which was successfully surgically removed through a small peritoneal defect; postoperative β-human chorionic gonadotropin levels declined appropriately, confirming complete evacuation. The case highlights that comprehensive diagnostic evaluation combining β-HCG measurement, transvaginal ultrasound, and cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI) is essential for diagnosing REP and preventing severe complications including life-threatening hemorrhage from rupture into the highly vascularized retroperitoneal space. Risk factors such as prior cesarean deliveries and induced abortions may predispose patients to this condition through tubal damage and abnormal retroperitoneal communications.

title: Abdominal pregnancy: a report of five cases and literature review
summary:
# Summary: Abdominal Pregnancy - A Rare and Life-Threatening Ectopic Condition

Abdominal pregnancy is a rare subtype of ectopic pregnancy (less than 1% of cases) wherein embryonic implantation occurs at extrauterine abdominal sites including the omentum, peritoneal surface, and pouch of Douglas, representing a significant gynecological emergency. Risk factors for this condition include congenital reproductive system malformations, prior ectopic pregnancy, fallopian tube surgery, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease. Clinical presentations are variable but commonly include lower abdominal pain, amenorrhea, vaginal bleeding, and nausea, with cases potentially progressing to life-threatening hemorrhage and demonstrating approximately eightfold higher mortality than tubal pregnancy. Diagnosis requires careful evaluation combining elevated hCG levels, ultrasonography detection of the yolk sac and embryo, and imaging with CT or MRI to localize the implantation site and distinguish abdominal pregnancy from intrauterine implantation. Treatment options include methotrexate, which inhibits gestational trophoblast proliferation, along with surgical intervention via laparotomy or laparoscopic surgery, with postoperative hCG monitoring essential for assessing treatment efficacy. Early diagnosis and intervention with appropriate surgical expertise significantly reduce maternal morbidity and improve clinical outcomes.

title: Heterotopic Pregnancy After Intracytoplasmic Sperm Injection With Viable Embryos: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

The widespread use of assisted reproductive technologies (ARTs), including in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection, has significantly increased the incidence of heterotopic pregnancy—the simultaneous occurrence of both intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies. Endometriosis is a major predisposing factor for heterotopic pregnancy, operating through multiple mechanisms including chronic inflammation, adhesions, and fibrosis that increase ectopic pregnancy risk; additionally, endometriosis itself affects approximately 40% of infertile women and causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. Key risk factors for heterotopic pregnancy in ART cycles include multiple embryo transfers, fresh embryo transfers, blastocyst-stage transfers, tubal abnormalities, reduced endometrial thickness, and suboptimal embryo transfer techniques. This case demonstrates that early transvaginal ultrasound diagnosis is critical for detecting heterotopic pregnancies and enabling timely surgical intervention such as laparoscopic salpingectomy, which can preserve the intrauterine pregnancy while removing the ectopic component. Clinical management recommendations emphasize single embryo transfer policies, careful assessment of risk factors including endometriosis status, and vigilant monitoring in ART patients to improve outcomes and reduce heterotopic pregnancy complications.

title: After the Shock: Impact of Ectopic Pregnancy on Subsequent Fertility and Parenthood
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, the implantation of a fertilized ovum outside the uterus typically in the fallopian tube, accounts for 1-2% of all pregnancies and represents a leading cause of maternal morbidity with risks including tubal rupture, internal bleeding, and hemodynamic instability. The condition significantly impairs subsequent fertility through multiple mechanisms: direct tubal damage, inflammatory processes causing fibrosis and adhesions, and compromised ciliary function following infection or surgical intervention like salpingectomy.
