Major risk factors include prior ectopic pregnancy (10-25% recurrence risk), pelvic inflammatory disease from Chlamydia or gonorrhea, prior tubal surgery, smoking, and advanced maternal age, while early diagnosis via transvaginal ultrasonography and serum β-hCG testing reduces morbidity and mortality. Medical management with methotrexate or conservative surgical approaches (salpingostomy) preserve tubal function better than salpingectomy, though infertility and recurrent ectopic pregnancy remain significant long-term concerns even after successful treatment. Beyond physical sequelae, ectopic pregnancy produces profound psychological consequences including grief, anxiety, and fear of recurrence that may alter reproductive decisions; comprehensive post-ectopic pregnancy care incorporating early fertility counseling, psychological support, and consideration of assisted reproductive technology optimizes both patient well-being and fertility outcomes.

title: Efficacy of Monotherapy Letrozole Versus Methotrexate for the Management of Ectopic Pregnancy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Comparative Studies
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis compared letrozole and methotrexate as medical treatments for ectopic pregnancy, finding comparable treatment success rates between the two agents. Letrozole achieves its therapeutic effect by inhibiting aromatase, thereby reducing placental estrogen and progesterone production while downregulating vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and steroid receptors—all of which disrupt trophoblastic cell proliferation and pregnancy maintenance. Methotrexate works through a different mechanism, inhibiting tetrahydrofolate to disrupt DNA synthesis and induce trophoblastic cell death. Although both treatments demonstrated similar efficacy, letrozole offered a more favorable safety profile, being associated with lower β-hCG levels on day 4, higher platelet counts, and reduced liver enzyme levels (AST and ALT), while methotrexate carried risks of serious adverse effects including liver disease, renal impairment, folate deficiency, and ovarian reserve reduction. These findings suggest letrozole represents a safer, better-tolerated alternative to methotrexate for managing ectopic pregnancy while maintaining comparable clinical effectiveness.

title: A Clinical and Risk Factor Profile of Patients With Tubo-Ovarian Abscesses: A Retrospective Study From Two District Hospitals in the United Kingdom
summary:
# Summary

Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA), a severe complication of pelvic inflammatory disease, causes significant morbidity in women of reproductive age by leading to infertility, ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and potentially life-threatening complications including sepsis and abscess rupture. The condition typically presents with fever, lower abdominal pain, and elevated inflammatory markers, though fever and leukocytosis may be absent in some cases, complicating diagnosis. Risk factors include obesity (present in 56.5% of patients), intrauterine device use, smoking, and diabetes, with obesity particularly compromising immune function and increasing inflammatory responses. Clinical and laboratory parameters, including abscess size ≥7.4 cm, CRP levels >143.5 mg/L on admission, and elevated fever, strongly predict treatment failure and the need for surgical intervention, with surgically managed patients demonstrating significantly larger abscesses, higher CRP levels, and prolonged hospitalization. While initial management involves broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics, abscess characteristics and patient factors such as older age and elevated BMI are critical predictors of antibiotic failure and the need for surgical management. These findings underscore the importance of prompt identification and risk stratification to guide appropriate management and prevent serious complications including recurrent pelvic inflammatory disease associated with anatomical factors such as endometriosis.

title: Schistosomiasis and ruptured ectopic pregnancy: A case report
summary: Schistosoma haematobium infection causes female genital schistosomiasis, which leads to chronic inflammation in the genital tract and deposits calcified eggs within Fallopian tubes. This egg deposition triggers fibrotic disruption and granuloma formation, which impairs tubal peristalsis and reduces luminal diameter, substantially increasing the risk of ectopic pregnancy—a complication that occurs at earlier ages in infected women compared to the general population. The resulting pathology can progress to extensive fibrosis and infertility if left untreated, though praziquantel therapy is effective when administered promptly. This case report highlights how female genital schistosomiasis led to a ruptured ectopic pregnancy requiring surgical intervention (salpingectomy) in a low-income setting where delayed diagnosis and misdiagnosis are common due to limited healthcare access and the nonspecific presentation of symptoms including vaginal discharge, pelvic pain, and abnormal menstrual cycling. The findings underscore the critical public health burden of schistosomiasis in resource-limited regions, where poor living conditions perpetuate transmission cycles and reproductive complications remain largely preventable through early diagnosis and treatment.

title: Prediction of ectopic pregnancy using interpretable machine learning algorithms
summary: This study develops interpretable machine learning algorithms to predict ectopic pregnancy (EP) by analyzing multiple clinical and demographic risk factors. Ectopic pregnancy presents with characteristic symptoms including unilateral lower abdominal pain and vaginal hemorrhage, and carries serious complications such as rupture and life-threatening bleeding. Key risk factors identified include prior history of EP (2.432-fold increased likelihood), genital surgery (2.392-fold increased likelihood), pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis (odds ratio 2.6), menstrual and ovulatory disorders, psychiatric conditions, advanced maternal age, smoking, and IUD use. Machine learning models, particularly Random Forest algorithms achieving 87.13% accuracy and 90.65% AUC, identified mid-cycle pain, dysmenorrhea, and vaginal bleeding as the strongest clinical predictors alongside surgical and psychiatric history. These findings demonstrate that interpretable machine learning approaches can significantly improve clinical decision-making and patient outcomes by accurately identifying women at elevated risk for ectopic pregnancy in diverse reproductive health contexts.

title: Tubal Stump Ectopic Pregnancy Following Salpingectomy: A Rare Diagnostic Challenge
summary: Tubal stump ectopic pregnancy is a rare but serious complication occurring in approximately 0.4% of ectopic pregnancies following salpingectomy, with tubal stump length and remaining intact tubal lamina emerging as potential risk factors for recurrence. Ectopic pregnancies broadly are responsible for 5-10% of pregnancy-related deaths and carry multiple established risk factors including prior ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, intrauterine device use, and smoking, with a 10-20% recurrence rate in women with previous ectopic pregnancy. Clinical diagnosis relies on standard ultrasound imaging and serial beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements, with advanced imaging such as MRI potentially offering improved localization of ectopic implantation sites. Ruptured ectopic pregnancies cause significant maternal morbidity through intra-abdominal bleeding, hemoperitoneum, and hemoglobin decline, presenting with classic symptoms of vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain that warrant high clinical suspicion. Management has evolved toward medical approaches using methotrexate, though surgical intervention with hemostatic devices may be necessary in ruptured cases, supplemented by pain management and blood product transfusion as needed. This case highlights the diagnostic challenge of tubal stump pregnancies and emphasizes the importance of maintaining awareness of this rare complication in women with prior salpingectomy.

title: ESR Essentials: Gynaecological causes of acute pelvic pain in women: a primer for emergent evaluation—practice recommendations by the European Society of Emergency Radiology
summary:
# Summary

This article provides clinical guidance from the European Society of Emergency Radiology on evaluating acute pelvic pain in women, with emphasis on imaging-based diagnosis of gynecological emergencies. The main conditions associated with acute pelvic pain include ectopic pregnancy, adnexal torsion, ovarian cysts, and pelvic inflammatory disease, all of which are effectively diagnosed using transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound as first-line investigations. Pelvic inflammatory disease, caused by ascending bacterial infection, can trigger serious complications including infertility, ectopic pregnancy, salpingitis, pyosalpinx, and tubo-ovarian abscess, with MRI demonstrating superior sensitivity (0.95) compared to CT (0.79) for detection. The article also highlights that endometriosis and endometriomas are associated with acute pelvic pain through rupture and superinfection, and describes additional causes including uterine leiomyoma degeneration, hemorrhagic ovarian cysts, and rare complications like Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome with perihepatitis. Early antibiotic treatment of pelvic inflammatory disease and prompt imaging identification of life-threatening complications are essential to prevent long-term sequelae and mortality.

title: Heterotopic pregnancy following ovulation induction with clomiphene citrate therapy: A case report
summary: This case report documents heterotopic pregnancy—the simultaneous occurrence of intrauterine and ectopic implantations—following clomiphene citrate therapy for anovulatory infertility. Clomiphene citrate, a selective estrogen receptor modulator used as first-line treatment for anovulatory infertility particularly in polycystic ovarian syndrome, stimulates polyovulation and increases the risk of heterotopic pregnancy through multiple mechanisms. The combination of clomiphene citrate-induced polyovulation and underlying PCOS (which increases tubal contractility via elevated estrogen and alters tubal motility) created a high-risk scenario for this rare but life-threatening condition. Additional risk factors for heterotopic pregnancy identified include prior tubal damage from pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and mechanical injury, as well as advanced maternal age and previous ectopic pregnancy history. The case emphasizes that while transvaginal ultrasound is highly sensitive for detecting ectopic pregnancy, early diagnosis of heterotopic pregnancy remains challenging, and clinicians should maintain heightened suspicion when managing pregnancies in patients on ovulation-inducing therapy. The patient required emergency laparoscopic salpingectomy, highlighting the potential severity of heterotopic pregnancy as a complication of fertility treatment.

title: A Case of Heterotopic Pregnancy Resulting in a Live Birth Through Laparoscopic Surgery
summary: Heterotopic pregnancy (HP), the simultaneous occurrence of intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy, is a rare but serious complication significantly associated with assisted reproductive technology and infertility treatments including clomiphene citrate, in vitro fertilization, and embryo transfer. Risk factors for HP overlap substantially with those for ectopic pregnancy and include tubal factor infertility, endometriosis, prior abdominal or tubal surgery, sexually transmitted diseases, and smoking. The condition presents a diagnostic challenge because the presence of an intrauterine pregnancy can obscure detection of the concurrent ectopic pregnancy on routine transvaginal ultrasound, potentially delaying treatment and increasing the risk of life-threatening intraperitoneal bleeding. This case demonstrates that laparoscopic surgery serves as both a diagnostic and therapeutic tool for HP, allowing definitive histopathological confirmation of tubal pregnancy and enabling safe removal via salpingectomy while preserving the intrauterine pregnancy. Although surgical intervention carries a risk of fetal loss, this patient achieved a successful live birth following laparoscopic management, illustrating that careful diagnosis and minimally invasive treatment can result in favorable outcomes even in this high-risk scenario.

title: Laparoscopic management of unruptured left ovarian ectopic pregnancy: a rare case report
summary: Ovarian ectopic pregnancy (OEP) is a rare form of ectopic pregnancy characterized by implantation of gestational tissue within the ovarian parenchyma, presenting with nonspecific symptoms including lower abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, amenorrhea, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Risk factors for OEP include pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, assisted reproductive techniques, intrauterine device use, and prior tubal surgery, with potential mechanisms involving secondary implantation of aborted tubal pregnancies or direct fertilization within ovarian tissue. The diagnosis is challenging because OEP is frequently misdiagnosed as benign ovarian pathology such as corpus luteum cysts or hemorrhagic cysts; while ultrasonography and β-hCG measurement support diagnosis, histopathology confirming the presence of chorionic villi and trophoblastic tissue within ovarian parenchyma remains the gold standard. OEP carries significant morbidity risk due to the ovary's abundant vascularity, which predisposes to rupture, internal hemorrhage, and hypovolemic shock, typically presenting acutely in the first trimester. Laparoscopic surgery is the preferred management approach, allowing direct visualization and targeted excision with ovarian preservation through wedge resection or cystectomy rather than oophorectomy, especially in women of reproductive age. Post-operative monitoring with serial serum β-hCG levels and follow-up ultrasound is essential to confirm resolution of the ectopic pregnancy.

title: Optical coherence tomography enables the depth-resolved measurement of cilia beat frequency in ex vivo human fallopian tubes
summary: This study demonstrates that optical coherence tomography (OCT) enables non-invasive depth-resolved measurement of cilia beat frequency in human fallopian tubes, providing a novel tool for assessing reproductive function. Fallopian tube cilia are critical for fertility, driving oocyte pickup, embryo transport, and sperm transport, while impaired cilia motility is linked to multiple reproductive pathologies including endometriosis, adenomyosis, ectopic pregnancy, and tubal factor infertility. The research shows that cilia beat frequency varies with patient-specific factors such as age, hormonal status, and concurrent pathology, and that infertility-causing conditions are associated with reduced beat frequency. OCT offers significant advantages over traditional microscopy by providing rapid, spatially-resolved imaging without requiring tissue staining, though the technique is susceptible to noise from mucus, blood cells, and temperature fluctuations. The method enables characterization of both ciliary dynamics and fallopian tube structural morphology, with potential applications for understanding how metachronal wave patterns regulate luminal content propagation. Future studies controlling imaging variables and examining menstrual cycle-dependent changes could improve the clinical utility of OCT for diagnosing and managing ciliary dysfunction-related infertility and endometriosis.

title: Association between ovarian maldescent and infertility: a prospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian maldescent is an independent anatomical abnormality that significantly impairs female fertility by causing prolonged conception times and reducing natural pregnancy rates, though the underlying mechanism involves abnormal positioning of the ovaries and fallopian tubes that prevents proper oocyte capture and transport. The condition is characterized by elongation of ovarian tissue, shortening of the suspensory ligament, and elongation of the fallopian tubes and utero-ovarian ligament, anatomical changes that may paradoxically elevate anti-Müllerian hormone levels and enhance ovarian reserve while simultaneously compromising reproductive function. Notably, patients with ovarian maldescent demonstrate substantially longer median duration of preparation for conception (7 months versus 3 months in controls) and carry increased risk for ectopic pregnancy due to impaired embryo transport through elongated fallopian tubes. In vitro fertilization successfully overcomes the fertility deficit associated with ovarian maldescent, with pregnancy rates comparable to controls when IVF is employed, suggesting that assisted reproductive techniques bypass the anatomical obstruction to natural conception. Management strategies include modified IVF protocols utilizing transabdominal oocyte retrieval and mild ovarian stimulation to reduce ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome risk in these patients, with surgical repositioning of the ovary considered in select cases.

title: Sequential intramural pregnancies in the right uterine cornu after the transfer of two embryos in a single cycle: a case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Intramural pregnancy (IMP), a rare subtype of ectopic pregnancy involving gestational sac implantation within the myometrium, poses a life-threatening clinical challenge by causing progressive myometrial thinning that leads to uterine rupture and severe hemorrhage. This case report documents sequential IMPs in the right uterine cornu following transfer of two fresh embryos in a single assisted reproductive technology cycle, highlighting multiple risk factors including adenomyosis, endometrial damage, and procedural factors such as excessive transfer fluid volume and deep embryo insertion. The high-estrogen environment during IVF enhances uterine smooth muscle contractions and alters endometrial receptivity through gonadotropin effects, while abnormal embryo viability and invasiveness further increase IMP risk. Clinical diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound showing heterogeneous echo areas with rich blood flow, supported by MRI revealing uneven signal intensity and cystic lesions, and confirmed by histopathological identification of chorionic villi surrounded by myometrial muscle tissue. Treatment approaches range from conservative management with methotrexate to laparoscopic surgical removal, with the case emphasizing that sequential IMP development despite intervention requires careful monitoring of HCG levels and consideration of mifepristone administration. This report underscores that ART-related intramural pregnancies demand heightened clinical suspicion given the nonspecific presentation of abdominal pain, irregular bleeding, and pelvic effusion.

title: Development and validation of a nomogram for predicting ectopic pregnancy in patients receiving double fresh cleavage stage embryo transfer
summary: This study developed and validated a nomogram to predict ectopic pregnancy (EP) risk in patients undergoing double fresh cleavage-stage embryo transfer during assisted reproductive technology. Multiple independent risk factors were identified, including tubal factor infertility (OR=1.680), previous tubal surgery particularly bilateral salpingostomy (OR=2.667), polycystic ovarian syndrome (OR=1.809), uterine malformation (OR=5.663), elevated serum estradiol levels, and reduced endometrial thickness on the day of human chorionic gonadotropin trigger (OR=0.585). Chronic endometritis was also significantly more prevalent in EP cases (17.1% versus 10.0%), suggesting endometrial factors contribute to ectopic implantation risk. The resulting nomogram effectively stratified patients into high and low EP risk groups, with the high-risk group experiencing significantly elevated EP incidence (OR=4.902), enabling individualized risk assessment and counseling for patients undergoing embryo transfer procedures.

title: Long-Term Reproductive Outcomes After Palmer-Type Neosalpingostomy in Hydrosalpinx: A Seven-Year Real-World Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Hydrosalpinx, a fluid-filled fallopian tube dilation commonly caused by pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, or prior pelvic surgery, is a major cause of female infertility that reduces implantation and pregnancy rates through multiple mechanisms including embryo washout, embryotoxicity, and impaired endometrial receptivity. Palmer-type neosalpingostomy, a microsurgical technique emphasizing atraumatic handling and meticulous mucosal preservation to restore fimbrial function, achieved a 33.8% clinical pregnancy rate and 25.6% intrauterine/live birth pregnancy rate in this seven-year cohort study, though recurrence occurred in 21.2% of cases. Disease severity and pelvic adhesions were the strongest predictors of success, with mild hydrosalpinx achieving 73.3% live birth rates compared to only 10.2% in severe disease, while extensive adhesions significantly reduced pregnancy likelihood. Best candidates for neosalpingostomy are women with mild-to-moderate tubal damage, preserved ovarian reserve, and minimal pelvic adhesions; notably, patient age, ovarian reserve markers, and infertility type did not predict outcomes. Although neosalpingostomy carries a 4.4% ectopic pregnancy risk and higher recurrence rates compared to salpingectomy, it preserves tubal anatomy and permits natural conception in carefully selected patients.

title: Methotrexate Use in the Management of Ectopic Pregnancies: A Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, most commonly implanted in the fallopian tube, presents with abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, and nausea, with multiple risk factors including prior ectopic pregnancy, pelvic surgery, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and maternal age over 35 years. Methotrexate, a folic acid antagonist that inhibits DNA synthesis in trophoblastic cells, is the primary medical treatment for hemodynamically stable patients with unruptured ectopic pregnancies, with success rates strongest when baseline β-hCG levels are below 4340 mIU/mL and associated favorable features include smaller masses (<2.0 cm), absence of fetal cardiac activity, and presence of yolk sac. Lower baseline β-hCG levels emerged as the strongest predictor of methotrexate success, whereas elevated baseline β-hCG levels significantly predicted both treatment failure and need for surgical intervention. Alternative management approaches include expectant monitoring for asymptomatic patients with very low β-hCG levels (below 200 mIU/mL), which demonstrated spontaneous resolution in 88% of cases, and surgical intervention via salpingostomy or salpingectomy for hemodynamically unstable patients or those with treatment failure. Transvaginal ultrasound and β-hCG trending remain essential diagnostic tools, with diagnostic laparoscopy reserved for equivocal cases when ectopic pregnancy suspicion remains high despite negative imaging findings.

title: Trojan horse in obstetric complications: A case report of concurrent uterine perforation, incarcerated fallopian tube and cesarean section scar pregnancy
summary: This case report describes a rare obstetric complication involving the concurrent occurrence of uterine perforation, fallopian tube incarceration, and cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP). Intrauterine procedures such as dilation and curettage (D&C) and hysteroscopy are well-established causes of uterine perforation, which can subsequently lead to fallopian tube incarceration through disruption of pelvic anatomy. Cesarean scar pregnancy, increasingly common with rising cesarean delivery rates, represents implantation of a gestational sac within a prior cesarean incision and carries the serious risk of uterine rupture and life-threatening hemorrhage. The diagnostic challenge in this case arose because fallopian tube incarceration and early-stage CSP present with indistinguishable clinical features, including elevated β-hCG levels and lateral uterine adnexal masses detected on imaging, making transvaginal ultrasound and MRI essential for definitive diagnosis. Emergency combined laparoscopic-hysteroscopic surgery successfully treated all three complications through resection of the CSP lesion, anatomical repositioning of the incarcerated tube, and repair of the uterine defect with hemostasis achieved via bipolar cautery and suture ligation. Postoperative fertility surveillance using serial transvaginal sonography, saline infusion sonohysterography, and hysteroscopy is recommended to assess endometrial cavity integrity and tubal patency in patients with future childbearing plans.

title: Acute Hemoperitoneum Secondary to Chronic Hematosalpinx: A Diagnostic Challenge in the Setting of a Negative Pregnancy Test
summary:
# Summary

Hematosalpinx, the accumulation of blood within the fallopian tubes, presents a significant diagnostic challenge with multiple etiologies including ectopic pregnancy, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal torsion, and malignancy. Endometriosis affecting the fallopian tubes causes cyclical hemorrhage from endometrial implants, leading to tube distension and hematosalpinx formation, while tubal torsion produces hemorrhagic infarction through compromised arterial supply and mechanical blockage of adnexal vessels. Rupture or leakage of chronic hematosalpinx can result in acute hemoperitoneum with progressive anemia and hemodynamic compromise, creating an acute surgical emergency that may occur even with negative pregnancy tests, making endometriosis a critical differential diagnosis in reproductive-age women. Imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, CT, and MRI are essential for diagnosis, and emergency laparoscopic salpingectomy effectively treats the condition by removing affected tissue and addressing ongoing hemorrhage. This case highlights the importance of considering non-pregnancy-related causes of hematosalpinx in patients with negative β-hCG levels presenting with acute abdominal pain and hemoperitoneum.

title: An individualized risk prediction tool for ectopic pregnancy within the first 10 weeks of gestation based on machine learning algorithms
summary: This study developed a machine learning-based risk prediction tool for ectopic pregnancy (EP) within the first 10 weeks of gestation, identifying multiple clinical and biochemical predictors of this life-threatening condition. The CatBoost algorithm achieved superior diagnostic performance (AUROC 0.930) by integrating key clinical features including vaginal bleeding patterns, serum progesterone and hCG levels, presence of homogeneous adnexal masses, abdominal tenderness, and obstetric history of cesarean section or pelvic surgery. Counterintuitively, lower progesterone and hCG levels, along with reduced vaginal bleeding relative to normal menstrual flow, were strongly associated with increased EP risk, reflecting underlying trophoblast dysfunction and hormonal imbalance stemming from abnormal embryo implantation in ectopic locations. The pathophysiological mechanism involves hormonal insufficiency impairing endometrial maintenance, leading to decidual tissue breakdown and the characteristic light vaginal bleeding observed in EP cases. This individualized prediction approach addresses a critical clinical need, as EP accounts for approximately 75% of early pregnancy-related deaths and can progress to life-threatening tubal rupture and hemorrhagic shock if diagnosis is delayed. The tool's integration of clinical presentation with biomarker patterns offers improved early detection and risk stratification for this obstetric emergency.

title: Analysis of perioperative fertility-related stress and associated factors in women of childbearing age undergoing salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy: a study based on the health ecology model
summary: Women undergoing salpingectomy for ectopic pregnancy experience substantial perioperative fertility-related stress driven by multiple interconnected factors across clinical, psychological, socioeconomic, and interpersonal domains. Unilateral salpingectomy significantly compromises reproductive capacity, with infertility risk reaching 50–60%, which is particularly distressing for women who have not yet achieved their desired family size and must simultaneously process both acute pregnancy loss and surgical fertility reduction. Key clinical predictors of elevated stress include older age, contralateral tubal pathology, and prior assisted reproductive technology use, while psychosocial factors such as strong fertility intentions, limited social support, lower income, and fewer existing children independently increase psychological burden. The study, employing the health ecology model framework, identified that traditional family fertility concepts and perceived social support represent important contextual variables that modulate perioperative stress responses in this vulnerable population. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive perioperative psychological support and counseling that addresses both the immediate trauma of ectopic pregnancy and the long-term reproductive implications of surgical management in women of childbearing age.

title: Non-Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI)-Related Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID)
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) arises from multiple pathogenic pathways, with both sexually transmitted organisms—including *Chlamydia trachomatis*, *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, and *Mycoplasma genitalium*—and non-STI bacteria such as those associated with bacterial vaginosis causing significant upper reproductive tract infection. Sexually transmitted pathogens directly damage tubal epithelium and cilia, while bacterial vaginosis-associated organisms including *Gardnerella vaginalis*, *Prevotella*, and *Atopobium* degrade cervical mucus and trigger cytokine-mediated fibrosis, compromising natural barriers. Non-STI PID commonly involves enteric and anaerobic bacteria (particularly *Prevotella* and *Atopobium*, found in 25–62% of cases), with *Fusobacterium nucleatum* emerging as a significant pathogen in chronic PID that suppresses immune clearance and resists treatment. Risk factors for PID include vaginal douching, intrauterine device use, gynecologic procedures that breach the endocervical canal (hysteroscopy, curettage, hysterosalpingography), and gastrointestinal comorbidities including inflammatory bowel disease. PID leads to severe long-term complications including chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy, with previous infections significantly increasing recurrence risk due to preceding tubal damage.

title: Copper Intrauterine Device Migration Into Cesarean Scar Niches Without Complications: A Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This case report describes a rare complication of intrauterine device placement in a patient with a prior cesarean delivery. A 28-year-old woman presented with lower abdominal pain and abnormal vaginal bleeding caused by copper IUD migration into a cesarean section scar niche. Transvaginal ultrasound identified the inverted IUD with one arm embedded within the cesarean scar defect, a finding confirmed during hysteroscopic examination and successfully treated by device removal without complications. The article emphasizes that while IUD displacement typically leads to serious outcomes including ectopic pregnancy, device expulsion, and uterine perforation, migration into cesarean scar niches is exceptionally uncommon and often underreported. Risk factors for IUD migration include small or irregular uterine cavities, strong uterine contractions, postpartum insertion timing, and improper insertion technique, with cesarean scar defects substantially elevating this risk. The case underscores the clinical importance of transvaginal ultrasound as the first-line diagnostic tool for evaluating suspected IUD displacement in symptomatic patients and highlights hysteroscopy as an effective treatment approach.

title: Rare Presentation of a Viable Secondary Abdominal Ectopic Pregnancy in the Rudimentary Horn of a Bicornuate Uterus: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare viable secondary abdominal pregnancy implanted in the rudimentary horn of a bicornuate uterus, which carries significant clinical risks. Rudimentary horn pregnancies are associated with high rates of rupture typically occurring between 14 and 20 weeks of gestation, leading to life-threatening intraperitoneal hemorrhage that makes diagnosis and management extremely challenging. Multiple factors increase the risk of abdominal ectopic pregnancy, including uterine deformities, assisted reproduction, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and prior tubal surgery. In this case, placental implantation location and adequate vascular supply enabled unusual embryonic survival, though abdominal pregnancies are associated with fetal malformations in up to 40% of cases and reduced neonatal survival rates. The patient underwent prophylactic bilateral common iliac artery ballooning and DJ stenting before midline laparotomy to manage the pregnancy, with supportive interventions including antenatal steroids for fetal lung maturation and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection. This case demonstrates the critical importance of early diagnosis and specialized multidisciplinary management for this life-threatening but rare obstetric emergency.

title: Frontier and Inequality Analyses of the Global Burden of Ectopic Pregnancy: Insights from the GBD 2021
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, characterized by blastocyst implantation outside the uterine cavity, represents a significant global health burden associated with maternal mortality and compromised future fertility. The condition arises from functional or anatomical impairment of fallopian tube transport, frequently linked to pelvic inflammatory disease, prior tubal surgery, and endometriosis, which disrupt normal embryo migration and lead to ectopic implantation in the fallopian tubes, cervix, ovaries, or abdominal cavity. While global age-standardized incidence rates declined from 305.9 to 212.87 per 100,000 between 1990 and 2021, absolute disability-adjusted life years increased from 288,083 to 396,856 due to population growth and epidemiological changes, with the highest burden shifting to the 20-24 age group. Diagnosis relies on serial beta-human chorionic gonadotropin monitoring and transvaginal ultrasonography, with management ranging from methotrexate therapy to surgical intervention based on hemodynamic stability. Prior ectopic pregnancy history significantly increases risk for recurrent ectopic pregnancy, infertility, preterm birth, and placental disorders. Sub-Saharan African nations demonstrate the largest unmet potential for health improvement, though global health inequalities in ectopic pregnancy burden are narrowing, emphasizing the need for equity-focused interventions that enhance early diagnosis, safe management, and fertility preservation.

title: Polymicrobial PID Presenting as Primary Peritonitis in a Young Immunocompetent Patient—Case Report and Disease Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a 21-year-old immunocompetent woman who presented with primary peritonitis caused by polymicrobial sexually transmitted infection, with cervical cultures identifying Chlamydia trachomatis, Mycoplasma hominis, and Ureaplasma parvum. Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are the most common bacterial causes of pelvic inflammatory disease, while Mycoplasma hominis—strongly associated with bacterial vaginosis—contributes significantly to upper genital tract inflammation, often as a co-infection. Ureaplasma parvum, typically causing asymptomatic infections, can rarely progress to peritonitis through chronic colonization. Untreated pelvic inflammatory disease leads to serious sequelae including tubal damage, adhesions, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and potential associations with endometriosis and genital cancers. The patient was successfully treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics including doxycycline (active against all three pathogens), combined with peritoneal lavage and subsequent outpatient therapy with doxycycline and amoxicillin-clavulanate, highlighting the importance of timely intervention to prevent long-term reproductive complications.

title: Ectopic Pregnancy with a Normally Located Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine System in a Woman with Adenomyosis: Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents an unusual occurrence of ectopic pregnancy in a woman with adenomyosis who had a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) in place, highlighting adenomyosis and LARC devices as previously underrecognized risk factors for extrauterine pregnancy. Adenomyosis causes multiple pathophysiological changes including disruption of the junctional zone, abnormal uterine peristalsis, and reduced endometrial receptivity through local vascular and inflammatory alterations, which collectively may predispose to ectopic pregnancy rather than normal intrauterine implantation. While the LNG-IUS effectively treats adenomyosis symptoms and related conditions including heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain through endometrial atrophy and decidualization, the case demonstrates that contraceptive efficacy against intrauterine pregnancy does not equivalently protect against ectopic pregnancy. The patient was successfully managed with laparoscopic salpingectomy, which offers advantages over open surgery including reduced blood loss and postoperative morbidity. This case emphasizes the importance of considering adenomyosis as a contributing factor when counseling women about ectopic pregnancy risk and selecting appropriate management strategies.

title: Cervical Ectopic Pregnancy After Frozen Embryo Transfer: Methotrexate Failure Managed With Uterine Artery Embolization and Ultrasound-Guided Curettage
summary: Cervical ectopic pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening complication of assisted reproductive technology that carries high risk of severe hemorrhage and fertility loss due to the cervix's rich vascularity and limited contractility. This case demonstrates methotrexate failure in a patient with multiple risk factors including elevated β-hCG (>14,000 mIU/mL), embryonic cardiac activity, and prior viable gestation—all established predictors of poor medical management response. The patient's underlying endometriosis, pelvic adhesive disease, tubal factor infertility, and technically difficult embryo transfer further increased her risk of abnormal implantation. Rather than proceeding to hysterectomy, the clinical team successfully preserved fertility using bilateral uterine artery embolization immediately followed by ultrasound-guided suction curettage, resulting in minimal blood loss and avoiding life-threatening hemorrhage that typically accompanies standard dilation and curettage in cervical pregnancies. This fertility-preserving approach, increasingly supported by case series evidence, represents an important alternative to hysterectomy and demonstrates that even when first-line methotrexate therapy fails in high-risk cervical ectopic pregnancies, surgical intervention can be performed safely with appropriate hemostatic strategies. The case highlights the critical role of transvaginal ultrasound in early diagnosis and the necessity of tailored management based on clinical risk factors and disease characteristics.

title: From Suspicion to Confirmation: An Original Study on a Complete Diagnostic Pathway for Ectopic Pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive diagnostic study establishes a complete clinical pathway for managing ectopic pregnancy (EP), which remains a major cause of maternal morbidity and mortality, particularly when rupture occurs in the first trimester. The research demonstrates that transvaginal ultrasonography combined with serial serum β-hCG monitoring enables accurate site-specific localization and classification of ectopic implantation across multiple anatomical locations, including tubal, cesarean scar, cervical, and ovarian pregnancies. Methotrexate effectively treats selected cases of tubal and cesarean scar pregnancy through mechanisms that reduce serum β-hCG levels by at least 15% at seven days, while surgical management via laparoscopy remains necessary for tubal pregnancies with hemorrhage and for cervical pregnancies requiring evacuation. Risk factors for EP include previous ectopic pregnancy, tubal pathology, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, and progestin-based contraceptive methods that impair tubal ciliary activity and peristalsis. The study confirms that increasing cesarean section rates and assisted reproductive technology use have elevated cesarean scar pregnancy and heterotopic pregnancy incidence, underscoring the need for individualized management strategies based on integrated clinical, biochemical, and imaging findings.

title: Natural Conception After Tubal Reconstruction: A Rare Success Following Bilateral Fimbrioplasty for Post-pelvic Inflammatory Disease Occlusion
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic inflammatory disease causes distal tubal occlusion and tubal factor infertility by damaging the delicate fimbrial epithelium, leading to hydrosalpinx formation that further impairs fertility. This case demonstrates successful natural conception following robotic-assisted bilateral fimbrioplasty in a carefully selected patient with bilateral distal occlusion, filmy adhesions, and preserved fimbrial tissue architecture. The procedure restored tubal patency, confirmed by chromotubation, and the patient achieved spontaneous pregnancy within ten weeks without resorting to in vitro fertilization. Success of tubal reconstructive surgery depends critically on patient selection factors including mild tubal damage, healthy fimbrial mucosa, minimal adhesions, preserved ovarian reserve, and normal semen parameters, while extensive fibrosis, older age, and recurrent infections reduce surgical success rates. Robotic-assisted microsurgery offers enhanced precision through three-dimensional visualization and tremor filtration, enabling atraumatic tissue handling that minimizes thermal damage to delicate structures and preserves ciliary function. Although ectopic pregnancy remains a notable risk complication occurring in 5-10% of post-fimbrioplasty cases, tubal reconstruction represents a cost-effective alternative to assisted reproductive technology for well-selected patients seeking natural conception.

title: Tubal Stump Ectopic Pregnancy After IVF-ET in Patients Who Underwent Salpingectomy or Adnexectomy: A Qualitative Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Tubal stump ectopic pregnancy represents a rare but serious complication following IVF-embryo transfer in patients who have undergone salpingectomy or adnexectomy, with the condition frequently complicated by heterotopic pregnancy (coexisting intrauterine and extrauterine gestations). The primary clinical manifestations include abdominal pain (71.7% of cases) and tubal rupture (69.6%), which occurs with dangerous hemoperitoneum in the majority of ruptured cases, creating life-threatening hemorrhage risk. Multiple risk factors contribute to ectopic pregnancy after IVF-ET, including maternal factors such as tubal disease, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and smoking, as well as technical factors including high transfer medium volume and multiple embryo transfers. Surgical intervention with tubal stump excision (74.1%) or cornuostomy (24.1%) represents the primary treatment, with laparoscopic approaches significantly reducing hospitalization time compared to laparotomy. Importantly, rupture of the ectopic pregnancy does not compromise intrauterine pregnancy outcomes in heterotopic cases, with 81.9% of intrauterine pregnancies resulting in delivery and 95.5% of singletons delivering at term. Early diagnosis through transvaginal ultrasound with color Doppler mapping, serial β-hCG dosage, and assessment of stump size and fetal viability is critical for preventing maternal morbidity and mortality.

title: Effective Local Methotrexate Therapy for Interstitial Ectopic Pregnancy: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: Interstitial ectopic pregnancy, which occurs when a fertilized ovum implants in the intramural segment of the fallopian tube within the myometrium, is a rare but life-threatening condition accounting for 2-4% of ectopic pregnancies with substantial risks of rupture, severe hemorrhage, and maternal mortality. Multiple risk factors including endometriosis, fibroids, pelvic inflammatory disease, prior ectopic pregnancy, cesarean delivery, and assisted reproductive technologies predispose to this condition, which typically presents with first-trimester vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain. Early diagnosis via transvaginal ultrasonography and elevated β-hCG monitoring enables conservative medical management with systemic or local methotrexate injection, which is enhanced by adjunctive mifepristone through promotion of decidual breakdown and increased trophoblast sensitivity to cytotoxic therapy. While methotrexate effectively treats stable cases, it carries risks of gastrointestinal symptoms, liver toxicity, and myelosuppression, whereas mifepristone can cause vaginal bleeding, uterine cramping, and hypersensitivity reactions. Surgical interventions including cornual wedge resection, cornuotomy, salpingectomy, and laparoscopic resection are reserved for cases with anatomical abnormalities, recurrence, or detectable fetal cardiac activity requiring intracardiac potassium chloride injection prior to medical therapy. This case report demonstrates that careful imaging, heightened clinical suspicion, and serial β-hCG monitoring have improved outcomes for conservative treatment of interstitial ectopic pregnancy in hemodynamically stable patients.

title: Evaluation of Letrozole and Methotrexate in the Management of Ectopic Pregnancy – A Randomized Clinical Trial Study
summary: This randomized clinical trial compared letrozole and methotrexate for medical management of ectopic pregnancy, evaluating their efficacy and safety profiles. Letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor that blocks peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogens, achieved a 70% success rate in treating ectopic pregnancy compared to methotrexate's 85% success rate, though letrozole reduced beta-human chorionic gonadotropin (β-hCG) levels more rapidly. The rationale for aromatase inhibition stems from estrogen's established role in implantation and embryonic development; by suppressing estrogen production, letrozole disrupts the hormonal conditions necessary to sustain ectopic pregnancies while potentially downregulating progesterone receptors. Notably, letrozole demonstrated a superior safety profile compared to methotrexate, which causes indiscriminate suppression of rapidly dividing cells leading to potential multi-organ toxicity, while letrozole's adverse effects remain minimal and manageable. The study supports letrozole as a promising medical alternative to surgery for ectopic pregnancy management, offering reduced morbidity risk while preserving fallopian tube integrity. These findings suggest aromatase inhibitors warrant further investigation as medical treatments for ectopic pregnancy, particularly given their favorable tolerability and potential application in other gynecological conditions including endometriosis and uterine myoma.

title: Functional Changes in the Fallopian Tube: Environmental Factors, Lifestyle, Pathological Conditions and Pharmacological Agents
summary:
# Summary

The fallopian tube maintains its critical functions in gamete transport and early embryo development through a delicate balance of hormonal, cellular, and mechanical regulation. Estradiol upregulates ciliary beat frequency and smooth muscle contractions while reducing tubal fluid viscosity, whereas progesterone produces opposing effects to prepare the tube for embryo transit. Endometriosis significantly impairs fallopian tube function through adhesion formation and direct damage to ciliary architecture and contractility, particularly in moderate-to-severe disease, thereby reducing sperm motility and binding capacity. Multiple environmental and infectious factors—including pesticides, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, smoking, alcohol, and bacterial pathogens like *Chlamydia trachomatis*—compromise tubal function through mechanisms ranging from epithelial damage to reduced ciliary beat frequency and muscle contractility, collectively elevating ectopic pregnancy risk. Certain medications, including SSRI antidepressants and levonorgestrel-containing contraceptives, also alter fallopian tube contractility and ciliary function in ways that may affect reproductive outcomes. These findings highlight how multifactorial perturbations of fallopian tube physiology—from hormonal imbalance to pathological conditions and pharmacological interventions—contribute to reproductive dysfunction and ectopic pregnancy risk.

title: Poster Session I - A128 THE CLINICAL UTILITY OF REFERRED SHOULDER PAIN FOR THE DIAGNOSIS OF INTRA-ABDOMINAL PROCESSES: A SYSTEMATIC REVIEW
journal: Journal of the Canadian Association of Gastroenterology
summary: Referred shoulder pain serves as a clinically useful diagnostic indicator for various intra-abdominal and peri-diaphragmatic pathologies. Diaphragmatic irritation is the primary mechanism causing referred shoulder pain, which can originate from diverse etiologies including ectopic pregnancy, diaphragmatic endometriosis, and exercise-related transient abdominal pain. The lateralization of shoulder pain provides additional diagnostic specificity, with left-sided pain associated with splenic pathologies such as abscess or colonoscopy-related injury, while right-sided pain correlates with cholelithiasis. Diaphragmatic endometriosis, a rare but important manifestation of endometriosis, presents with referred shoulder pain as a key clinical feature alongside variable presentations from other intra-abdominal processes. This systematic review establishes that the presence of referred shoulder pain can meaningfully guide clinical diagnosis and next diagnostic steps in evaluating patients with suspected intra-abdominal disease. Understanding this pain referral pattern is particularly relevant for gynecological conditions like endometriosis and ectopic pregnancy, where shoulder pain may be an overlooked diagnostic clue.

title: The diagnosis and management of extrauterine and uterine ectopic pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, with the majority occurring in the Fallopian tubes, represents a significant clinical challenge associated with substantial hemorrhagic morbidity and multiple etiological risk factors. Key risk factors include endometriosis, tubal damage from infection (particularly chlamydia and gonorrhea), previous pelvic surgery, smoking, advancing age, and history of prior ectopic pregnancy, with levonorgestrel emergency contraception and certain intrauterine contraceptive devices showing elevated association. The management spectrum has evolved to include expectant management and medical treatment with methotrexate, though laparoscopic surgery remains the optimal intervention for extrauterine ectopic pregnancies and laparoscopic salpingectomy for tubal cases. The underlying pathophysiology involves impaired tubal motility through reduced ciliary beat frequency (mediated by progesterone and interleukin-6), enhanced tubal receptivity to implantation via interleukins 6 and 8, and increased angiogenic signaling through WNT2B produced by trophoblasts, with the anti-inflammatory hormone adrenomedullin playing a protective role by suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines. Assisted reproductive technologies, particularly those involving multiple embryo transfers, higher-risk embryo stages, and thin endometrium, significantly increase ectopic pregnancy risk, while frozen-thawed embryo transfers appear protective.

title: Beyond Inflammation: A Comprehensive Outlook on the Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine Treatment of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
summary: Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is a complex inflammatory condition of the upper genital tract primarily caused by sexually transmitted infections and microbial pathogens, presenting clinically with fever, lower abdominal pain, abdominal distension, abnormal vaginal discharge, and bleeding. Untreated PID can lead to serious sequelae including tubal factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain, with the underlying pathology involving microbial infection, multiple immune dysregulation, and progressive fibrosis. While Western medicine effectively controls acute infection through antibiotics, emerging resistance and high recurrence rates have prompted exploration of integrated treatment approaches combining Western and traditional Chinese medicine. Traditional Chinese medicine mechanisms include regulation of Th17/Treg immune balance, reduction of inflammatory factors and tissue fibrosis, and improvement of local microcirculation, as validated through oral compound formulas, modified classical prescriptions, and external treatments. Integrated traditional Chinese and Western medicine treatment demonstrates superior clinical outcomes by achieving multi-target synergistic effects that improve clinical symptoms, reduce recurrence rates, and preserve reproductive function better than either approach alone.

title: Emergency management of recurrent ovarian ectopic pregnancy in a hemodynamically unstable patient: A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report examines emergency management of recurrent ovarian ectopic pregnancy in a hemodynamically unstable patient, highlighting a rare but life-threatening condition. Ovarian ectopic pregnancies account for less than 3% of all ectopic pregnancies but pose significant morbidity and mortality risk due to the thin ovarian cortex's limited elasticity, which almost invariably leads to rupture and catastrophic intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Risk factors for this presentation included previous ectopic pregnancy, endometriosis, pelvic adhesions, and intrauterine surgical procedures. The patient presented with hemodynamic instability, acute abdomen, and progressive anemia despite initial transvaginal ultrasonography showing no intrauterine gestational sac, necessitating emergency exploratory laparotomy that revealed massive hemoperitoneum and a gestational sac on the right ovary surface. Histopathological examination confirmed chorionic villi in the resected ovarian tissue. This case underscores the critical importance of early diagnosis with high-resolution ultrasonography and recognition of recurrence risk in patients with prior ectopic pregnancies, as surgical intervention is essential for hemodynamically unstable presentations when conservative management with methotrexate is contraindicated.

title: A rare case of a retroperitoneal pregnancy: case report
summary: Retroperitoneal ectopic pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening condition associated with multiple risk factors including endometriosis, prior myomectomy, intrauterine device use, and abdominal trauma. The pathophysiology involves fertilized oocytes migrating through compromised lymphatic capillaries or implanting on endometriotic lesions, with subsequent trophoblastic invasion causing bleeding and organ rupture through proximity to major retroperitoneal vessels. Clinical presentation includes elevated β-HCG levels, anemia, and characteristic imaging findings of a gestational sac in the retroperitoneal space with active hemorrhage. Emergency surgical intervention with tranexamic acid administration to reduce bleeding is essential for managing this condition, and postoperative monitoring of β-HCG levels is critical for detecting residual trophoblastic tissue. This case highlights the importance of recognizing retroperitoneal pregnancy as a differential diagnosis in women with risk factors presenting with hemoperitoneum and elevated β-HCG despite an empty uterus.

title: Molecular insights into salpingitis-induced infertility and ectopic pregnancy: pathogenesis, biomarkers, and translational perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Salpingitis, primarily caused by sexually transmitted pathogens such as *Chlamydia trachomatis* and *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, triggers complex inflammatory cascades that drive both tubal factor infertility and ectopic pregnancy through overlapping pathogenic mechanisms. Acute infection initiates neutrophil infiltration, while persistent inflammation progresses to chronic disease characterized by lymphocyte and macrophage infiltration, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), and sustained epithelial injury. The resulting pathology includes tubal scarring, ciliary dysfunction, fibrosis, adhesions, and epithelial cell layer disruption that collectively impair gamete transport and embryo movement, predisposing to both infertility and ectopic implantation. Emerging pathogens including *Mycoplasma genitalium*, *Ureaplasma urealyticum*, and loss of protective *Lactobacillus* species further increase susceptibility to salpingitis. Although antibiotics effectively treat acute infection, they fail to prevent chronic complications; thus, identifying early biomarkers of epithelial transformation and understanding the transition from acute to chronic disease are essential for developing therapeutic strategies to mitigate permanent reproductive damage.

title: Laparoscopic cornuostomy combined with methotrexate intralesional injection for conservative management to interstitial pregnancy: a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates that laparoscopic cornuostomy combined with intralesional methotrexate injection provides safe and effective conservative treatment for interstitial pregnancy, a rare but dangerous form of ectopic pregnancy associated with endometriosis, uterine leiomyoma, and pelvic inflammatory disease. Methotrexate functions as an antifolate antimetabolite agent that inhibits DNA synthesis and arrests trophoblast growth, while intralesional injection minimizes systemic toxicity compared to systemic administration. Early ultrasound diagnosis using criteria such as the interstitial line sign is critical for identifying candidates suitable for medical management, as deep myometrial implantation commonly leads to delayed diagnosis and increased rupture risk. The laparoscopic approach reduces uterine trauma compared to traditional wedge resection, which carries a 30% incidence of uterine rupture in subsequent pregnancies. In this case, β-hCG levels declined to negative within one month following treatment, and future pregnancies should be delivered via caesarean section to prevent rupture at the prior cornuostomy site. Treatment success depends on multiple factors including pre-operative β-hCG levels, gestational sac diameter, and presence of fetal cardiac activity, making early diagnosis essential for reducing morbidity and mortality.

title: Primary Peritoneal Pregnancy Requiring Conversion From Laparoscopic to Open Surgery Due to Massive Hemorrhage: A Case Report
summary: Primary peritoneal pregnancy is a rare form of ectopic pregnancy with a high maternal mortality rate of approximately 5.1 per 1,000 pregnancies, as illustrated by this case of massive hemorrhage requiring emergency surgical conversion. Risk factors for peritoneal pregnancy include intrauterine contraceptive device use, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and assisted reproductive technologies. During laparoscopic surgery attempted to treat suspected ectopic pregnancy, rupture of trophoblastic tissue implanted on the peritoneal surface resulted in life-threatening hemorrhage requiring 2,450 mL blood loss and transfusion. Histopathological examination confirmed chorionic villi, and imaging revealed a gestational sac in the pouch of Douglas. This case emphasizes that early peritoneal pregnancy can cause unexpected massive hemorrhage and that prompt conversion from laparoscopy to open surgery is critical for controlling bleeding in hemodynamically unstable patients. Preoperative magnetic resonance imaging should be considered when clinical presentation is compatible with peritoneal implantation, as early diagnosis may facilitate safer management of this rare but life-threatening complication.

title: Heterotopic pregnancy in a patient with severe hypothyroidism: a rare case report
summary: This case report presents a rare combination of severe hypothyroidism and heterotopic pregnancy (simultaneous intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy), highlighting previously underrecognized connections between endocrine dysfunction and abnormal implantation. While heterotopic pregnancy occurs spontaneously in fewer than 1 per 10,000 pregnancies, assisted reproductive technology and ovulation induction markedly increase its incidence to approximately 1%, with established risk factors including pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, previous ectopic pregnancy, and intrauterine device use. Thyroid hormones regulate critical processes in early pregnancy including angiogenesis and trophoblastic invasion; severe untreated hypothyroidism impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including anovulation, luteal phase defects, hyperprolactinemia, and reduced endometrial receptivity, potentially contributing to abnormal embryo implantation. The patient required emergency laparotomy and salpingectomy due to massive hemoperitoneum, emphasizing that heterotopic pregnancy carries substantial maternal morbidity and mortality risk. Transvaginal ultrasonography between 5–8 weeks, enhanced by color Doppler imaging, remains the diagnostic cornerstone despite persistent false negative rates. This case suggests thyroid dysfunction may represent a previously unrecognized contributor to heterotopic pregnancy pathogenesis, warranting further investigation into the role of endocrine dysfunction in abnormal implantation.

title: The Predictive Value of Systemic Inflammatory Markers from Failed Single-Dose Methotrexate Therapy in Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy: A Retrospective Single-Center Study
summary: This retrospective study evaluates systemic inflammatory markers as predictors of single-dose methotrexate (MTX) treatment failure in tubal ectopic pregnancy. Methotrexate treats ectopic pregnancy by inhibiting trophoblast proliferation through blockade of folic acid metabolism, inducing trophoblast apoptosis and alleviating pathological inflammation. Multiple day-4 biomarkers significantly correlated with treatment failure: elevated β-hCG (>304.8 mIU/mL or increases >35.7%), elevated lymphocyte counts (>2.165 × 10⁹/L), and reduced neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) decline (<40.3%), with initial hCG >342.25 mIU/mL also predictive of failure. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio reflects systemic immune-inflammatory status and effectively discriminates between successful and failed MTX treatment, with reduced NLR associated with therapeutic success. A predictive nomogram incorporating these inflammatory markers with β-hCG demonstrated moderate discriminative performance at day 4 for identifying patients at risk of methotrexate resistance, potentially enabling early intervention before ectopic mass rupture and associated life-threatening complications.

title: Hepatic Ectopic Pregnancy: A Diagnostic Challenge Highlighted by Multimodal Imaging
summary:
# Summary

Hepatic ectopic pregnancy represents an extremely rare form of abdominal pregnancy associated with high vascularity and significant risks of maternal morbidity and mortality, making diagnosis exceptionally challenging due to nonspecific clinical symptoms and the hepatic lesion's variable ultrasound appearance. This case illustrates how elevated β-hCG levels without intrauterine pregnancy visualization necessitate multimodal imaging—including transvaginal ultrasound, MRI, and hepatic ultrasound—to differentiate hepatic ectopic pregnancy from mimics such as gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. Conservative medical management with methotrexate was selected for this hemodynamically stable patient with a small subcapsular lesion, avoiding the significant hemorrhage risk associated with surgical intervention in the highly vascular liver. The diagnosis was supported by the absence of chorionic villi in endometrial sampling, steady β-hCG decline following methotrexate administration, and complete resolution of both the hepatic lesion and β-hCG elevation on serial imaging over five months. This case underscores the critical importance of coordinated multidisciplinary care, thorough biochemical monitoring, and advanced imaging in managing rare ectopic pregnancies, while highlighting how conservative approaches with methotrexate may be viable in appropriately selected patients.

title: Emergency Surgical Management of Ectopic Pregnancy Using Transvaginal NOTES (vNOTES) versus Laparoscopy: A Comparative Single-Surgeon Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic pregnancy, which occurs in over 90% of cases within the fallopian tube, remains a significant cause of maternal morbidity and mortality, with delayed diagnosis or intervention leading to severe complications including hemoperitoneum, acute anemia, and hemodynamic instability. This study compared transvaginal natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery (vNOTES) to laparoscopy for emergency surgical management of ectopic pregnancy, demonstrating that vNOTES significantly reduces operative time, procedure-related blood loss, and hemoglobin decrease compared to standard laparoscopic approaches. The mechanistic advantage of vNOTES arises from posterior colpotomy providing direct access to the pouch of Douglas, the lowest point of the pelvis where blood preferentially accumulates, enabling spontaneous drainage of hemoperitoneum and rapid clearance of the operative field for improved visualization. Beyond these perioperative benefits, vNOTES offers additional advantages including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, and importantly, the absence of visible abdominal scars that improves body image and patient satisfaction while aligning with enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) principles. Both salpingectomy and salpingostomy techniques successfully treat ectopic pregnancy via vNOTES, with hemostatic interventions such as mesosalpinx coagulation and selective bipolar cauterization readily performed without prolonged aspiration required in laparoscopic cases. However, vNOTES is contraindicated in patients with severe pelvic adhesions or advanced-stage endometriosis that obliterate the pouch of Douglas.

title: Recurrent Ectopic Pregnancies Following Bilateral Partial Salpingectomy: A Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary: Recurrent Ectopic Pregnancies Following Bilateral Partial Salpingectomy

This case report demonstrates that partial salpingectomy carries a significantly higher risk of recurrent ectopic pregnancy compared to complete salpingectomy, primarily due to residual tubal segments retaining physiologic function. The authors identify that tubal remnants longer than 4 centimeters can undergo recanalization, creating a conduit for sperm passage while blocking normal embryo transport to the uterus, thereby promoting ectopic implantation in the fallopian tube. Young maternal age and African-American race emerged as additional demographic risk factors, with younger patients showing 3.5 times higher cumulative probability of post-sterilization ectopic pregnancy over ten years. The case highlights established risk factors including pelvic inflammatory disease, prior ectopic pregnancy, pelvic surgery, endometriosis, and use of assisted reproductive technologies. Management of acute ectopic pregnancy involves diagnostic laparoscopy with definitive treatment through salpingectomy or salpingo-oophorectomy. The authors recommend consideration of total salpingectomy when feasible to eliminate recurrence risk and reduce long-term morbidity, particularly in reproductive-aged women.

title: Ruptured Ovarian Ectopic Pregnancy Managed Laparoscopically With Subsequent Term Delivery After 2 years Follow‐Up: A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian ectopic pregnancy (OEP) is a rare but serious condition associated with multiple risk factors including pelvic inflammatory disease, intrauterine contraceptive device (IUCD) use, endometriosis, in vitro fertilization, and previous adnexal surgery. The proposed mechanism involves IUCD-induced changes in prostaglandin synthesis that increase tubal motility, promoting abnormal ovarian implantation, while primary OEP can also result from ovulatory dysfunction. Transvaginal ultrasound is the diagnostic gold standard, revealing characteristic features such as an empty uterus, thickened endometrium, free pelvic fluid, and the hypervascular "ring of fire" sign on Doppler imaging. Laparoscopic surgical resection with diathermy is the preferred treatment approach, offering advantages including reduced intraoperative blood loss, fewer postoperative adhesions, shorter hospitalization, and lower analgesic requirements compared to open surgery. This case report demonstrates successful laparoscopic management of a ruptured ovarian ectopic pregnancy with subsequent term delivery after two years, supporting the favorable fertility outcomes reported following surgical management, with conception rates between 64-70% within two years post-treatment.

title: First Study on Antimicrobial Susceptibility and Molecular Epidemiology of Neisseria gonorrhoeae Isolates from Infertile Women
summary:
# Summary

Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes ascending infections from the lower to upper female reproductive tract, leading to pelvic inflammatory disease and serious reproductive complications including infertility and ectopic pregnancy in women. This study examined antimicrobial susceptibility patterns in N. gonorrhoeae isolates from infertile women and found alarming resistance rates, with 100% ciprofloxacin resistance, 98.1% tetracycline resistance, 79.3% penicillin resistance, and 19.8% azithromycin resistance, while ceftriaxone resistance remained at 2.8% with an additional 16% showing decreased susceptibility. The predominant molecular sequence types were ST5061 and ST4539, and resistance originated from multiple independent genetic sources. Despite ceftriaxone remaining effective below the WHO 5% resistance threshold, treatment failures have been documented with ceftriaxone-azithromycin dual therapy, leading the USA and UK to revert to ceftriaxone monotherapy. The high prevalence of asymptomatic gonorrhea (82.1%) in infertile women with multidrug resistance (78.3% resistant to three or more antibiotics) underscores the urgent need for antimicrobial surveillance to prevent transmission of resistant strains and reduce long-term reproductive complications from this WHO-designated high-priority pathogen.

title: Uncontrolled Diabetes as the Sole Risk Factor in a Rare Candida glabrata Tubo-Ovarian Abscess
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of Candida glabrata tubo-ovarian abscess in a patient with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus as the sole identifiable risk factor. Uncontrolled diabetes impairs immune function through multiple mechanisms, including reduced neutrophil function, tissue ischemia, and disruptions to the vaginal microbiome, while simultaneously enhancing Candida virulence through glucosuria that provides nutrients for fungal colonization. The patient's extensive vaginal colonization with C. glabrata progressed to abscess formation despite empiric antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone, doxycycline, and metronidazole), highlighting that standard bacterial treatment regimens do not cover fungal pathogens. Identification of C. glabrata through abscess culture guided escalation to micafungin, an echinocandin superior to fluconazole against this species known for azole resistance, combined with laparoscopic drainage that achieved clinical resolution. This case underscores the importance of considering fungal tubo-ovarian abscess in diabetic patients who fail to improve with antibiotics within 48–72 hours and obtaining cultures to guide antifungal selection, particularly as non-albicans Candida species increasingly cause invasive infections in immunocompromised populations.
